Like legit, if you were to have one gram of protein or one gram of carbohydrates, you are getting four calories. But if you were to have one gram of fat, you are getting nine. So how much more food could I eat for that same amount of calories? That's one of the things that's going to change the game when it comes to you actually losing weight. Now, the third thing that I did was reframe how and when I use carbs. I don't consider myself a low carb person. I eat carbs because I understand that carbohydrates are my body's preferred energy source. When you eat carbs, your body breaks them down into glucose or blood sugar. So, I know that if I'm going to be doing a big hefty workout, I got a busy day, I'm going to need more carbs and more blood sugar to give me the energy that I need. Conversely, on days that I'm going to be hanging out at the house, you know, maybe I'm going to church, I'm going to be watching TV, I don't need as many carbohydrates. So, really keeping that in mind when it comes to how and when I'm going to eat carbs was a game changer for me. So, I'm going to show you how I approach meal prepping in light of knowing there are some days I'm going to need more energy and more carbohydrates and some days I am not going to need as many. So, I would have this one with the brown rice on one of my high carb days and I would have one of these one with the cauliflower rice and I don't need as many carbs. So, you don't need to avoid carbs, but kind of think of carbs as gas in the gas tank. Days when you're going to be going on a road trip, we want to fully fuel that gas tank. On days when you are going to be, you know, maybe just doing around the corner, you're going to the grocery store, you don't need as much. Now remember, when you eat carbs, your body breaks them down into glucose or blood sugar. And ideally, you want to keep your blood sugar as stable as possible. So, by eating foods that are higher in fiber, the chances of the blood sugar staying stable, baby, you're in good shape because that fiber is like a buffer that's going to prohibit the sugar blood sugar from spiking up really, really high. I also am looking at the amount of sugar that I'm having. So, I'm going to do my best at things that are high in fiber, low in sugar, and as close to nature as possible. And then I am really kind of always gauging how much carbs I'm going to based off my activity level. - What Can You Eat on a Low Carb Diet? (Full Food List): Kind: captions Language: en low carb diets are becoming increasingly popular but which foods are low in carbohydrates I will quickly go through healthy low carb food choices so you understand what you can eat on a low carb diet the best way for you to quickly classify whether a food is low carb is to think about its source did it come from an animal or a plant and that's significant because plants are the living organisms that make carbohydrates if you remember learning about photosynthesis that is the process that plants use to make carbohydrates from raw materials of sunlight carbon dioxide and water so all plant foods will contain carbs but that doesn't rule them out of a healthy little carb diet you just need to pick the right ones most foods that come directly from animals contain only trace amounts of carbs so let's start our low carb food list with meats meats can come from any source including beef poultry pork lamb venison and bison and the very versatile egg is a great addition to a low carb diet and works great as a snack meal or a recipe ingredient there are also products that come from meats that can count as low carb foods like bacon pork rinds beef jerky and lunch meats but as with any food processing lowers the nutrient quality of the food so you will be happiest with the overall quality of your diet if you limit processed meats and choose them wisely to avoid unhealthy ingredients as well as hidden sugars which will increase the carb count now I am going to discuss more than 100 low carb foods in this video so if you would like the full list you can download the list on my website and I will leave some links here for where you can do that ok let's continue all varieties of fish and seafood from wild caught salmon and haddock to shrimp and scallops can be enjoyed as part of a low carb diet now dairy products are the category of animal-based foods that you will want to pay attention - when counting carbs and there are two reasons first dairy contains a natural sugar called lactose and dairy foods are often sweetened which can of course increase the carb count of the food now while it doesn't apply 100% of the time a general rule of thumb is that the higher a dairy food is in fat the lower it will be in the milk sugar lactose for instance 1/4 cup of heavy cream has about one and a half grams of carbohydrates whereas the same amount of fat free skim milk has twice the carb grams that also provides flavor so full fat dairy foods don't need to rely on added sugars and sweeteners like their low fat counterparts so the takeaway is read your labels and don't be afraid to choose the full fat low sugar varieties when it comes to selecting dairy products the dairy foods that can fit into a low carb diet include full fat yogurt heavy cream half-and-half sour cream and different varieties of cheese but again pay attention to the carb count of Dairy Foods and we could round out our list of low carb animal-based foods with cooking fats those that come from animals include butter lard and tallow this also provides a perfect segue into plant-based low carb foods because we get some oils from plants including avocado oil which is one of my favorites as well as coconut oil and olive oil and there are also oils classified as a vegetable oils such as soybean sunflower and canola oil these oils fit on a low carb list but I do not use them due to their questionable health value and if you want to know more about that you can see my video on the best cooking oils as I mentioned earlier all whole plant foods contain carbs but that doesn't rule them out of a healthy low-carb diet you just need to pick wisely so that you're getting the vital nutrients from the plant foods without the excess carbs so let's continue our list of low carb plant foods and I'm going to loosely list them from lowest carb content to highest coffee NT come from coffee beans or tea leaves and contain only a trace amount of carbs making them a favorite drink of low-carb dieters herbs and spices also come from plants so they contain trace amounts of carbohydrates which is typically a non-issue because spices tend to be used in small quantities but you do want to read the label of packaged spice blends that have other ingredients added in for instance this packet of taco seasoning mix contains 4 carbs per serving and there's about six servings in here leafy green vegetables are a great low carb choice and many other non starchy vegetables will fit into your low carb lifestyle as well a few of the more popular low carb vegetables include asparagus green beans broccoli cauliflower mushrooms and onions onions are a bit higher in carbs than the other vegetables that I mentioned so that is something that you might want to pay attention to if you are following a very low carb or keto style diet nuts and seeds qualify as low carb foods but it pays to monitor how many you are eating to add crunch to a salad and can be blended into recipes to enhance their health value I prefer to eat nuts and seeds raw to avoid the unhealthy oils that are typically used for roasting some favorites are chia hemp sunflower and pumpkin seeds with the popularity of low carb diets we are seeing a rise in popularity of butter derived from nuts and seeds these natural kinds of butter have a place on our list but they are even easier to overeat than nuts and seeds themselves you have to read the label also to make sure that other ingredients including sugar were not added we can also grind not and similar plant foods to give us flour like almond flour or coconut flour for baking and while these flours may have some health benefits when compared to white flour anytime you refine a whole food it changes the way your body handles it so if you are using a low-carb diet as a way to lose weight you will be happiest with your overall results if you avoid or limit all types of flour - Calculating A Calorie Deficit For Weight Loss Over 40: Kind: captions Language: en Getting into a calorie deficit isn't as complicated as most people think. I'm gonna explain how you can start calculating a calorie deficit for weight loss. Hi, it's Ivana, helping you get fit healthy and strong at any age. If that sounds good, please subscribe to my channel. A calorie is a unit of energy. So if you wanna lose weight, then you'll want to burn off more calories than your body needs to maintain itself. If you give your body more calories than it needs on a daily basis, then you'll be in a calorie surplus, and you'll start to store fat. There are a lot of strategies for weight loss, but they all rely on getting into a calorie deficit because that's how you lose fat. You don't actually have to calculate a calorie deficit in order to lose weight. And I've got another video coming up where I'm gonna explain another strategy that you can use if you don't wanna calculate. So make sure you subscribe to my channel, but if you do wanna track and monitor your calories so that you know that you're as accurate as you possibly can be. The first thing that you need to do is determine maintenance calories. That's the amount of calories that your body needs just to maintain the weight that you're at right now. Now that means there's gonna be no gain in weight and no loss in weight. And this can vary by about 200 or 300 calories a day. It's kind of a moving target. It's not like your maintenance calories are static. So all the calculations that we're doing here are obviously just an estimate, but they do give you a good guideline to work from. Most of the calculators that you're gonna find online, that help you determine your maintenance calories are going to be a bit of an overestimate. So to keep things simple, I like to use a really easy multiplication: just 13 times, your body weight in pounds, in order to determine your maintenance calories. For someone who's very active, it might be 14 or 15 or 16 times your weight in pounds to determine your maintenance calories. I prefer to stick with that 13 times your body weight in order to get a more conservative estimate. So let's start there. So if you're 180 pounds, your maintenance calories are gonna be about 2,340 calories. How much weight do you wanna lose? So safe and effective weight loss is considered to be about one to two pounds per week. Now I love math, but I know people wanna keep these things as simple as possible. You can also estimate it by saying 0.8 to 1% of your body weight per week. And this is how we account for the fact that someone who's much larger can lose weight faster than someone who's already close to their target weight. If you're 300 pounds, you can safely lose up to three pounds per week. But if you're only at 150 pounds, you probably only wanna lose up to 1.5 pounds per week. You'll probably find that you'll lose weight faster at the beginning when you're a bit heavier and further from your goal than when you get closer to your goal weight. So let's use a very simple example. Let's assume that you wanna lose one pound of weight a week. That's realistic and practical. So that equates to about 3,500 calories per week. That you're going to have to deduct from what you are consuming, or you're gonna have to burn them off. Now, if we divide that by seven days, you're looking at 500 calories per day in order to lose that pound a week. So you'll need a 500 calorie deficit per day in order to lose a pound a week. Changing your diet alone will work really well if you're on the heavier side. So let's say you weigh about 250 pounds. That means that your maintenance calories would be 3250 calories per day. Dropping 500 calories a day from your diet means that you've been consuming 2,750 calories per day. And that's pretty reasonable. That's a decent amount of food, but let's say you're a smaller person who only weighs 150 pounds. That means that your maintenance calories are 1,950. And if you wanted lower that by 500 calories in order to get that deficit, then you're down to 1,450 calories a day. And that's not a lot. There's a general minimum energy intake that we consider to be reasonably healthy in order to provide your body with the micronutrients. And that's the vitamins and minerals that you need in order to keep functioning and to keep healthy for men, we consider that lower range 1800 calories per day. And for women, it would be about 1200 calories per day. And that's the absolute minimum of calories that you should be consuming. So forget those 800 calorie a day diets. Please! If you don't weigh as much, and you're a smaller person, it's a little bit more challenging to lose weight. That's also why as you lose weight, it becomes harder and harder. There's only so many calories you can drop down to. It's easier to lose weight when you have more weight to lose. So if you have 50 pounds to lose, you're gonna find those first 30 or 40 pounds a lot easier to take off than the last 10 pounds. When you get closer to your target weight, you're gonna have to fine tune things a little bit more when you get relatively lean. And I'm gonna talk about how someone who's more advanced and very close to their goal can make changes. Ideally, you wanna use a combination of diet and exercise in order to produce weight loss. So you want to reduce your calories in and increase your calories out. When I do online coaching, I tend to look at both sides of the equation for each particular person. And I wanna find the way that's going to be easiest for that person. What's going to be the best way of them achieving the calorie deficit. And what's gonna cause them the lowest amount of discomfort to change. Being more active can certainly increase your calorie deficit and help you to lose weight. But there's a limit on that. And it's gonna depend on whether you are currently sedentary, whether you're moderately active or you're very active already. So if you're inactive right now, mostly sedentary in your job, and you're not doing any workouts or formal exercise, then you have the most space in order to increase the calorie deficit through your physical activity. If you're moderately active, there's certain things you can play with. But if you're already very active in that you're walking a lot. You have, let's say three to five workout sessions in the gym every week. There isn't as much for you to work with. And chances are, if you are that active, you're gonna be fairly close to your target weight anyway. So that means you have to be much more specific and you'll probably need to track your calories more carefully in order to make sure that you continue to be in a calorie deficit and get leaner. So let's go back to that 500 calorie a day deficit that we want. And ideally, as I said, it's best to split those up 250 calories of activity, and then reducing our caloric intake by about 250 calories per day. So you can burn off those 250 calories a day through a formal workout. It'll probably take about 45 minutes. I mean, a lot of this depends on your body weight and how intense you are with the physical activity that you're doing. If you're working harder, you're gonna burn more calories. If you're working longer, So let's say you're at the gym or working out at home three to five days a week. That's probably going to be enough for those days. And on alternate days, you can also increase your activity by walking, let's say 30 to 45 minutes. You might not have time to do your workout and walking on the same day, but you wanna make sure that you're trying to burn off approximately 250 calories a day. If you're very busy during the week and just attempt to walk for about 30 minutes a day, and then you can make up some of that calorie burn on the weekend, because we're really looking at a weekly goal of 3,500 calories. If you don't burn off any additional calories one day, you can make them up on another day. But don't get too carried away and just do nothing for five days and then hope that you're going to be able to make up the activity on the weekend. That's not likely to work. I personally like to do additional activity on the weekends, whether it's going for a walk or doing some kind of sport with my family. That's how I burn off additional calories, probably more than I have time to do during the week. So if you're playing tennis for an hour, you're going for a long hike or a long bike ride, then potentially you could burn off 500 calories on a Saturday and 500 calories on Sunday. That also means that you won't have to worry about reducing your caloric intake on the weekend so much. And that tends to be when a lot of people over eat or at least eat a little bit more in a calorie surplus, and don't get that deficit just from their nutrition. Now, reducing your calories in by 250 calories a day, generally, isn't that hard to do. If you have a donut, you just skip that donut for the day. Sometimes those can be 300 calories. So reducing the amount of treat foods overall during the week and then on the weekends as well, can help just whittle those calories down. You only need to reduce your calorie intake by 250 calories. If you are doing additional exercise as well. Once you've been in a calorie deficit of approximately 3,500 calories a week for about three weeks, that's when you wanna make sure that you check your progress. I mean, you do wanna monitor along the way. It is helpful to weigh yourself. It is helpful to take a look at how your clothes fit, whether you feel leaner, whether you see any difference in the mirror, remember that there are natural variations in your weight from day to day or week to week. So you don't always wanna get caught up just with the weight loss. If you start to see changes leanness in your arms, your legs, particularly around the waist, that tends to be a good indicator for a lot of people. Then your calorie deficit is likely working and you can keep it going. Now you might have to make adjustments. If you don't see any of those changes. If after two to three weeks, you're really not seeing any signs of progress. And you've been trying to eliminate those 500 calories per day. Then you want to adjust and work your way down. It doesn't actually mean that you're doing a greater calorie deficit. Chances are that your estimates are a bit off. Maybe you're forgetting certain things that you're eating. Maybe you're not burning off as many calories because you're not pushing yourself as hard as you can during your workout. Or maybe you're not walking at the pace that you think you're walking. Remember that everything is always an estimate. So if you are not seeing changes, then it means you've estimated incorrectly. If you've actually been tracking and monitoring your calories with an app, like myfitnesspal, where on a day to day basis, you're entering in all your food and you see that the numbers are where you think your calorie deficit should be. Whether you've decided to go 500 calories less per day, because it made sense for you or whether you've decided to reduce your calorie intake by 250 calories a day, and then burn off an additional 250 calories through physical activity. You're going to see if you're in the right calorie deficit based on the results that you're achieving. So you may not be entering the foods correctly, or you might not be getting the quantities, correct. Or you just might be missing something, perhaps there's fats and sauces and things that you're not adding to your totals. So that's when it pays to take a closer look and start to bring those calories down again. I've already mentioned that it takes more effort as you get closer to your target weight. So if you're down to the last five or 10 pounds of fat that you wanna lose, you wanna get quite lean, then you really have to be specific. Maybe you need to be more focused on the intensity of your cardio so that you know that you're getting it high enough, or you need to go a little bit longer with your cardio sessions, or you need a weight training workout that's pushing you a little bit harder. So there are a lot of different ways that you can approach both sides of it for the calorie side. It tends to be just trying to be much more accurate and absolutely everything that you eat has to go into the app so that you can have an accurate number. And if it seems like you're doing that, but you're still not getting the result, then it looks like you might just be one of those people who needs a lower calorie intake in order to lose weight. And that's what you'll have to do unless you decide to work a little bit harder, add an extra workout each week, do a little bit more walking. It's always up to you how much you want to do. I just prefer the mix of nutrition and exercise because it's just a healthy, balanced lifestyle. It tends to go together. It makes you feel better. It is possible to do just diet alone or just to exercise alone. Although exercise alone is very challenging and you need to commit to working out quite a lot most days in order to burn off sufficient calories. Let me know in the comments, if there's anything you didn't understand, or if you'd like me to help you figure out your calorie deficit correctly. Please hit the like button. - 3-Day EATING PLAN to Start or Restart Low Carb Dieting: Kind: captions Language: en-US Whether you got off track or your&nbsp; low carb diet failed to launch,&nbsp;&nbsp; having a plan keeps you from spinning your wheels. This video shares a simple and&nbsp; specific eating plan that you&nbsp;&nbsp; can follow for the next three days&nbsp; to jumpstart your low carb success. [Intro Music] I am often asked to be specific&nbsp; and mention actual foods to eat.&nbsp;&nbsp; The challenge in doing this for a worldwide&nbsp; audience is that the food choices will not&nbsp;&nbsp; work for everyone. If you prefer a plant-based&nbsp; diet or have food restrictions, I am sorry that&nbsp;&nbsp; this video is not for you. However, I have many&nbsp; videos you can benefit from on my channel page. When you switch to a low carb diet, what&nbsp; you do not eat is as important as what you&nbsp;&nbsp; do eat. You want to avoid foods that stimulate&nbsp; your appetite and eat foods that quiet hunger.&nbsp;&nbsp; Let's start with what to avoid. Sugar is quick energy. When you eat it, it&nbsp; gives you an energy boost that feels good.&nbsp;&nbsp; This is one of the reasons eating sugar&nbsp; makes you want to keep eating sugar. The&nbsp;&nbsp; problem is that you cannot survive on&nbsp; a high-sugar diet. Although some of us,&nbsp;&nbsp; including my former self, have given it a good&nbsp; try. At some point, you will not feel good. Ironically, getting away from sugar can&nbsp; also lead to ill feelings. When you go from&nbsp;&nbsp; a high-sugar diet to no sugar, your blood&nbsp; sugar crashes, your energy level plummets,&nbsp;&nbsp; and you may even feel shaky. This happens because&nbsp; the human body runs on what it has available. When&nbsp;&nbsp; sugar is constantly coming in, your body runs on&nbsp; sugar. When you go low carb, you cut out sugar,&nbsp;&nbsp; so it must use an alternate energy source.&nbsp; The good news is that the alternative is fat,&nbsp;&nbsp; so you will become a better fat burner. The&nbsp; temporary bad news is that it will take time&nbsp;&nbsp; for your body to create the enzymes and pathways&nbsp; needed to efficiently burn fat for energy. So,&nbsp;&nbsp; your energy level will drop before it goes up,&nbsp; but this transition is well worth the effort. For the next three days, you want to&nbsp; eliminate added sugar. Specifically,&nbsp;&nbsp; avoid packaged foods and drinks with sugar&nbsp; listed as one of the first three ingredients.&nbsp;&nbsp; That is a long list because it includes nearly&nbsp; all of the ultra-processed foods out there. And&nbsp;&nbsp; unfortunately, ultra-processed foods make up&nbsp; over half of the total calories Americans eat. So, this one rule eliminates a lot of foods,&nbsp; which is okay because those are the foods that&nbsp;&nbsp; make us sick and fat. And you want to keep&nbsp; in mind that sugar goes by many names. So,&nbsp;&nbsp; if the food has corn syrup, dextrose,&nbsp; maltodextrin, fruit juice concentrate, sucrose,&nbsp;&nbsp; or another sugar alias listed as one of the first&nbsp; three ingredients, leave it at the grocery store.&nbsp;&nbsp; If you are unsure if sugar is hidden in a food,&nbsp; assume that it is and leave it at the store. A piece of fruit has natural sugar.&nbsp; That is okay because the sugar is&nbsp;&nbsp; locked within the fibrous portions of&nbsp; the fruit, slowing its absorption. So,&nbsp;&nbsp; you do not have to stop eating fruit of any&nbsp; variety to follow this 3-day eating plan. Two things that you will want to&nbsp; avoid, in addition to added sugar,&nbsp;&nbsp; are bread and breaded foods. You can find&nbsp; bread that has "zero net carbs" listed on&nbsp;&nbsp; the label or no sugar added. However, these&nbsp; commercial products do not deliver on the&nbsp;&nbsp; promise they make. Bread, along with breaded&nbsp; and fried foods, stimulate your appetite,&nbsp;&nbsp; defeating the overarching goal of this preparation&nbsp; period which is to feel in control of your eating. As you get more sophisticated with&nbsp; your low carb diet, you will find&nbsp;&nbsp; ways to make acceptable homemade versions&nbsp; of these foods, but save that until you&nbsp;&nbsp; feel in control of your eating, and you&nbsp; will be much happier with your results. Speaking of homemade. Home cooking&nbsp; is a key to successfully launching&nbsp;&nbsp; or relaunching your low carb&nbsp; diet, and it does not need to&nbsp;&nbsp; be complicated or time-consuming. So&nbsp; let's start building our eating plan. Start by hard-boiling six eggs. You will eat&nbsp; two each day. When you eat them is up to you.&nbsp;&nbsp; If you'd like to have breakfast, eat them in&nbsp; the morning. If you like to shorten your eating&nbsp;&nbsp; window to practice intermittent fasting,&nbsp; you can have them as an afternoon snack. Here again, this plan contains specific&nbsp; foods that do not work for everyone. However,&nbsp;&nbsp; if you can eat eggs, they just are not&nbsp; your favorite, I encourage you to still&nbsp;&nbsp; give this plan a try. The foods I am&nbsp; including were selected because they&nbsp;&nbsp; stabilize your blood sugar and satisfy&nbsp; hunger for hours. That stability has a&nbsp;&nbsp; calming effect, so you do not need to rely&nbsp; on willpower to feel in control of eating. Lunch is a large salad with protein, fat,&nbsp; and some fiber. The reason a salad works&nbsp;&nbsp; so well is because there is a lot to eat,&nbsp; so your stomach is filled with non-starchy&nbsp;&nbsp; vegetables that take a long time to digest. Salad&nbsp; is also the perfect base for adding protein, fat,&nbsp;&nbsp; and fiber. All four of these elements, volume,&nbsp; protein, fat, and fiber, give you sustained&nbsp;&nbsp; hunger control and blood sugar stability, which&nbsp; sets you up for success with your low carb diet. I have a recipe for my typical lunchtime&nbsp; salad, and I will provide a link in the&nbsp;&nbsp; description area below this video.&nbsp; If you just want to build your own,&nbsp;&nbsp; good salad toppers include slices of avocados,&nbsp; raw nuts and seeds, cheese, meat, fish, chicken,&nbsp;&nbsp; and full-fat salad dressing, which can&nbsp; be as simple as olive oil and vinegar. For dinner, you are going for the same&nbsp; hunger-satisfaction effect that you&nbsp;&nbsp; went for at lunch. Therefore, you want to have an&nbsp;&nbsp; entree that contains protein and fat and&nbsp; a side dish that adds volume and fiber. For this plan, make my Easy Keto&nbsp; Chicken Tenders. Meal prep is as&nbsp;&nbsp; easy as putting all of the ingredients in a&nbsp; bag, smooshing them together, and dumping them&nbsp;&nbsp; onto a baking sheet. I will link to the free&nbsp; downloadable recipe in the description area. With that recipe, I share how to make&nbsp; an easy side dish using a bag of frozen&nbsp;&nbsp; broccoli topped with cheese. It is simple&nbsp; to prepare because all you need to do is&nbsp;&nbsp; follow the directions on the bag and add&nbsp; butter and cheese. For that small effort,&nbsp;&nbsp; you add hunger-satisfying volume,&nbsp; fat, and fiber to your meal. You can make life simple by making a double&nbsp; batch of chicken tenders and eating them each&nbsp;&nbsp; day or mix up dinnertime by buying a frozen&nbsp; package of hamburgers and cooking them to have&nbsp;&nbsp; on one or two of the days. Yes, quality matters&nbsp; and grass-fed beef is your best choice, but if&nbsp;&nbsp; that is a barrier for you, look for 100% ground&nbsp; beef burgers so you can get yourself started. The burger will be eaten without a bun. You&nbsp; can wrap it in lettuce leaves if you would&nbsp;&nbsp; like to pick it up and add tomato, onion, and&nbsp; pickles. There are low-sugar ketchup varieties,&nbsp;&nbsp; but you will still be better off going&nbsp; with low-carb condiments, like mustard and&nbsp;&nbsp; real mayonnaise, preferably made with&nbsp; avocado oil, like Primal Kitchen brand.&nbsp;&nbsp; Miracle Whip is not a good choice because&nbsp; it is made with high fructose corn syrup. If you would like a different&nbsp; side dish than broccoli,&nbsp;&nbsp; pick up a bag of green beans and prepare&nbsp; them using the package directions. Will these be the most exciting meals? No. But&nbsp; they taste good, control your blood sugar and&nbsp;&nbsp; hunger, and fill your stomach, which takes&nbsp; us to the next part of our plan, eat enough. We have been conditioned to equate&nbsp; dieting with low calories. Calories&nbsp;&nbsp; are an important consideration for weight&nbsp; loss. However, cutting calories to eat less&nbsp;&nbsp; of the same appetite-stimulating foods&nbsp; leaves you with nothing but misery,&nbsp;&nbsp; and we do not keep doing things that make&nbsp; us miserable, at least not voluntarily. The goal of this 3-day plan is to prepare your&nbsp; body for weight loss. This should be looked at&nbsp;&nbsp; as a time to help your body feel comfortable.&nbsp; That comfort comes when your blood sugar and&nbsp;&nbsp; hunger hormone levels are stable. That stability&nbsp; comes from eating whole foods that have volume,&nbsp;&nbsp; protein, fat, and fiber. If I sound&nbsp; like a broken record, it is intentional. During this 3-day period, eat big portions&nbsp; at mealtimes. Salad fits in this bowl,&nbsp;&nbsp; top it with 4 to 6 ounces of meat, along with the&nbsp; other toppers that I mentioned and that you enjoy. Dinner, if you want an extra portion,&nbsp; eat it. Do not eat dessert. You want to&nbsp;&nbsp; utilize this prep period to move away from&nbsp; the idea that dessert must follow a meal. Those two hard-boiled eggs can be eaten as a&nbsp; small meal before lunch or in the afternoon,&nbsp;&nbsp; so you have three eating occasions during the&nbsp; day. While it might not always be possible,&nbsp;&nbsp; work on eating enough at mealtimes and eliminate&nbsp; snacking in between. But if you need something,&nbsp;&nbsp; I have videos on low carb and keto&nbsp; snacks. A few choices include raw or&nbsp;&nbsp; lightly toasted almonds, cheese,&nbsp; and no-sugar-added beef sticks. Diets often fail because they are&nbsp; a knee-jerk reaction to something.&nbsp;&nbsp; For instance, a friend says, Do you&nbsp; want to go on a diet with me? "Or,&nbsp;&nbsp; you feel uncomfortable in your body&nbsp; and say to yourself, enough is enough." Those moments can initiate action, but if you&nbsp; do not have a plan in place, it is easy to get&nbsp;&nbsp; sidetracked, eat too little, or eat the wrong&nbsp; foods. One day of this uncertainty is okay.&nbsp;&nbsp; Two days can be managed with willpower, but&nbsp; more than that leads to havoc and misery,&nbsp;&nbsp; and the diet fails before&nbsp; the rewards are realized. This 3-day eating plan will get you to the&nbsp; starting line. A well-thought-out low carb&nbsp;&nbsp; diet can get you to the ultimate prize, which&nbsp; is a new way of eating that is easy-to-follow,&nbsp;&nbsp; enjoyable, and effective. When&nbsp; those three E's are in place,&nbsp;&nbsp; there is no need or desire&nbsp; to return to old habits. If you need more motivation and direction&nbsp; to get started, download my free 0,1,2,3&nbsp;&nbsp; strategy. It gives you the basics of the eating&nbsp; plan we just discussed and comes with a video&nbsp;&nbsp; series that is not available on YouTube that&nbsp; explains how to get those three E's in place.&nbsp;&nbsp; You can get those resources by going&nbsp; to DrBeckyFitness.com/Free. Thanks&nbsp;&nbsp; for watching, and have a great rest of your day.

In fact, you will have more energy burning fat because it has over double the amount of calories per gram plus when your cells go through a process called ketogenesis it supressses ghrelin (the hunger hormone) so you will be less hungry as the insulin spikes will be far less and as a result so will inflammation as opposed to carbohydrates being your main fuel source. Your body is very clever and will find balance. So if your main fuel source is fat and the body requires additional glucose the liver will make this via a process called gluconeogenesis - which means generating new glucose. The problem with fuelling your cells with carbs from starch and sugar is that the cells can only accept so much. The receptors will then downregulate. So when these receptors reject insulin (which is made when the body breaks carbs down into glucose) the excess is then stored in your fat cells and also in your bloodstream when the cells are full. You then gain weight and will acquire numerous health problems in your organs and arteries. There is a process called glycation which happens when glucose attaches to proteins (including collagen and elastin proteins) and lipids which is responsible for a number of diseases such as diabetes and is also responsible for chronic inflammation and aging. So, even if you maintain weight loss through rigorous exercise and/or calorie restriction you will still suffer with health problems at some stage due to excess insulin and inflammation. One last note, if you do ever crave bread or a sweet treat/dessert etc it's very easy to make. I love making bread, desserts etc. In fact, one can pretty much eat most of what a western diet consists of, you just have to make everything from scratch with alternative ingredients and have a little patience as opposed to buying already made inflammatory food. Life isn't all sunshine and rainbows. Nobody said it was. It is work. Hard work. But if you plan your work and work your plan it becomes easier and therefore more enjoyable which calls to mind an adage Jim Rohn said which was very apt: "There are two types of pain you will go through in life. The pain of discipline or the pain of regret". I know which one I chose

- The Truth About Low Carb Diets for Women Over 40 (Are They Worth It?): Kind: captions Language: en Thinking about cutting carbs to lose weight? Be careful. Low carb diets aren't always the answer after 40. They can actually slow down your metabolism. When you drastically cut carbs, your body starts breaking down muscle for energy. Less muscle means a slower metabolism, making fat loss harder in the long run. The fix: Keep complex carbs like quinoa, oats, and sweet potatoes in your diet. Aim for at least 100 gram of carbs per day to support muscle function. Pair carbs with protein and healthy fats to balance blood sugar. A bowl of quinoa salad with veggies and chicken is way better than skipping carbs entirely. Low carb can also increase stress and cravings. When carbs are too low, your cortisol, that's your stress hormone, rises. High cortisol makes you store fat, especially around the belly, and can trigger cravings for sweets. The fix: Don't go below 100 g of carbs. It's not worth the stress. Choose whole, unprocessed carbs to keep your mood and metabolism stable. Avoid quick fixes like sugary energy bars or drinks. Include berries or a small sweet potato in your meal to curb cravings. And here's a biggie. Carbs are important for hormone balance. Carbs help produce serotonin, which regulates mood and sleep. Too few carbs can lead to irritability, poor sleep, and even more cravings. The fix: Eat carbs earlier in the day to boost mood and energy. Combine carbs with protein at dinner to support restful sleep. Try whole grains like brown rice or barley to keep your body balanced. A small serving of oats with Greek yogurt can help you feel satisfied without overdoing it. Most people think cutting carbs is the key to fat loss, but after 40, it's about the right carbs, not zero carbs. Low carb diets might sound like a quick fix, but they can hurt your metabolism, mood, and muscle mass after 40. Instead, focus on balanced meals that include smart carbs. Like this video if you've ever struggled with low carb diets. Subscribe for more realistic weight loss tips for women over 40. Hit that subscribe button now and let's eat smarter

- 10 MUST-HAVE Foods That Stop Muscle Loss in Seniors ( Sarcopenia Prevention Diet ): Kind: captions Language: en If you're over 60 and noticing your strength slipping away, don't panic. You've just stumbled onto something most people never find in time. Sarcopenia, the age related loss of muscle, doesn't have to be your future. And the fix may already be in your kitchen. You're incredibly lucky to have found this video because most seniors are fighting muscle loss in silence, not knowing that certain foods can actually slow it down or even reverse it. I'm Dr. Thomas Reed. And in the next few minutes, I'll walk you through the 10 most powerful foods that help stop sarcopenia in its tracks. These are sciencebacked, senior approved, and simple to add to your daily meals. Let's get started because the sooner you nourish your muscles, the longer they'll carry you. Let's start with the most powerful muscle building food on the planet, eggs. If your muscles could choose one food to eat every single day, they would choose eggs without hesitation. Each whole egg delivers 6 g of complete protein, which means it contains every single amino acid your muscles need to repair and grow stronger. But what makes eggs truly extraordinary for rebuilding your muscle mass is an amino acid called leucine. Think of leucine as the key that starts your muscle building engine. Your aging muscles become more resistant to building new tissue, but leucine forces them to pay attention and start the rebuilding process. Here's something most people get wrong about eggs. They throw away the yolks. Don't make this mistake. The yolk contains vitamin D, which your muscles absolutely require to function properly. Without adequate vitamin D, your muscles become weak and unresponsive, no matter how much protein you eat. You should eat two to three whole eggs daily for optimal muscle building. Cook them gently, softboiled, poached, or scrambled over low heat. This preserves the delicate protein structure that makes eggs so effective. Have them for breakfast with whole grain toast or add hard-boiled eggs to your afternoon salad. The beauty of eggs is that they start working immediately. Within hours of eating eggs, your muscles begin receiving a steady stream of amino acids that fuel the rebuilding process. This is why eggs should be your first line of defense against muscle loss. While eggs are working their magic in your system, our second muscle building powerhouse takes the rebuilding process to the next level. Greek yogurt is like having a muscle building laboratory in a convenient cup. A single serving contains 20 g of protein. That's more than three whole eggs worth of muscle building power. What makes Greek yogurt particularly brilliant for your aging muscles is that it contains two different types of protein that work together like a perfectly orchestrated team. Whey protein gets absorbed quickly, immediately flooding your muscles with amino acids. Quin protein releases slowly over several hours, providing a continuous supply of muscle building materials. This dual protein system is exactly what your muscles need as you age. Your body becomes less efficient at using protein. So having both fast and slow acting proteins ensures that your muscles stay in building mode for hours after eating. Greek yogurt also delivers calcium and vitamin D in a form that your body can easily absorb. Your muscles need calcium to contract properly, and vitamin D deficiency is one of the hidden reasons why many seniors feel weak and unsteady. Choose plain Greek yogurt over flavored varieties, which are loaded with sugars that can actually interfere with muscle building. Add fresh berries for natural sweetness, or mix in a tablespoon of ground flax seed for omega-3 fatty acids that reduce muscle inflammation. The protein in Greek yogurt is so effective that research shows it can help seniors regain muscle mass even without exercise. Though combining it with physical activity amplifies the results dramatically. Building on the protein foundation from eggs and Greek yogurt, our third muscle building champion brings something extra to the table that your aging muscles desperately need. Salmon and other fatty fish. A 4 oz serving of salmon provides 25 gram of complete protein. But it's the omega-3 fatty acids that make this fish truly special for muscle rebuilding. As you age, your muscles develop chronic inflammation that acts like a break on the muscle building process. This inflammation makes it harder for your muscles to respond to protein and exercise. The omega-3 fatty acids in salmon, particularly EPA and DHA, are like turning off that inflammatory break and allowing your muscles to function like they did when you were younger. Recent breakthrough research has shown that omega-3s can actually restore the muscle building response in older adults to levels similar to younger people. This means that when you eat salmon, you're not just getting highquality protein. You're getting nutrients that help your body use that protein more effectively than it has in years. Salmon is also one of the few foods that naturally contains meaningful amounts of vitamin D. Many seniors are severely deficient in vitamin D without knowing it. And this deficiency can cut your muscle strength by up to 25%. Just two servings of salmon per week can significantly improve your vitamin D status. The key is choosing wild caught salmon when possible as it contains higher levels of omega-3s and fewer contaminants than farm-raised varieties. If fresh salmon isn't always available, canned salmon and sardines are excellent alternatives that are budget friendly and convenient. Don't overlook other fatty fish like mackerel, herring, and tuna. These fish provide the same muscle building benefits and can add variety to your meals while keeping your muscles in constant rebuilding mode. While fish provides the omega-3s your muscles crave, lean beef delivers something equally important that most other proteins can't match. A complete package of muscle building nutrients that work together in ways that will surprise you. A 3oz serving of lean beef provides 22 g of complete protein. But it's the additional nutrients that make beef a muscle building superstar. Lean beef is one of the richest sources of creatine in the diet. You might think creatine is just for young athletes, but research shows it's particularly beneficial for seniors. Creatine helps your muscles produce energy more efficiently, which means you can maintain your strength and power as you age. This is crucial because muscle power, the ability to generate force quickly, declines even faster than muscle mass as you get older. The iron in beef is particularly important for your muscles. Iron carries oxygen to your muscle cells, and iron deficiency can leave you feeling weak and fatigued even if you're eating adequate protein. The iron in beef is hemi iron, which your body absorbs much more efficiently than the iron found in plant foods. Beef also provides zinc in amounts that are difficult to get from other foods. Zinc is essential for protein synthesis and muscle repair. Even mild zinc deficiency can significantly impair your body's ability to build and maintain muscle mass, making adequate zinc intake crucial for seniors. Choose lean cuts like sirloin, tenderloin, or round cuts to get all these muscle building benefits while keeping saturated fat moderate. Grass-fed beef contains higher levels of omega-3 fatty acids and other beneficial nutrients compared to grain-fed beef. The key to preparing beef for optimal muscle building is using gentle cooking methods like slow cooking, brazing, or grilling at moderate temperatures. These methods keep the meat tender and preserve the protein quality while making it easier to digest. Following the powerful punch of lean beef, our fifth muscle building food might seem humble. But cottage cheese is actually one of the most strategically important foods for rebuilding your muscle mass. One cup contains an impressive 28 g of protein, making it one of the most protein dense foods you can eat. What makes cottage cheese particularly valuable for your aging muscles is its unique protein composition. Cottage cheese is primarily casein protein which digests slowly and provides a steady release of amino acids for up to 7 hours after eating. This extended release is crucial for seniors because your muscles need a constant supply of amino acids to stay in building mode. This slow release property makes cottage cheese perfect for eating before bedtime. While you sleep, your body goes into repair mode, and having a steady supply of amino acids available prevents your muscles from breaking down during the overnight fast. Many seniors unknowingly lose muscle mass during sleep because they don't have adequate amino acids available for repair and maintenance. Cottage cheese is also exceptionally rich in calcium and phosphorus, both of which are essential for muscle contraction. The calcium in cottage cheese is particularly well absorbed because it's paired with protein and other nutrients that enhance absorption. This is important because calcium absorption decreases with age, making it crucial to choose calcium sources that your body can actually use. The versatility of cottage cheese makes it easy to incorporate into your daily routine. Eat it straight from the container for a quick protein boost. Mix it with berries for a satisfying snack or use it as a base for dips and spreads. For maximum muscle building benefit, try cottage cheese with a small amount of fruit before bed. The combination provides both slow digesting protein and a small amount of carbohydrates that can improve protein utilization. Choose full fat cottage cheese when possible as the additional calories and fat soluble vitamins can be beneficial for seniors who may be underweight or have difficulty absorbing nutrients. While the previous foods have been primarily protein sources, our sixth muscle building champion brings something different to your plate that your muscles need just as much. Quinoa, the ancient grain that's actually a complete protein powerhouse. What makes quinoa revolutionary for muscle building is that it's one of the few plant foods that contains all nine essential amino acids in the right proportions. One cup of cooked quinoa provides 8 g of complete protein, but it's the combination of protein and complex carbohydrates that makes quinoa particularly effective for rebuilding muscle mass. The carbohydrates in quinoa help drive amino acids into your muscle cells, making the protein more effective than if you ate it alone. Quinoa is exceptionally rich in magnesium, a mineral that many seniors are deficient in without realizing it. Magnesium is involved in over 300 reactions in your body, including muscle contraction, protein synthesis, and energy production. When you're magnesium deficient, your muscles can't contract properly, and the muscle building process slows down significantly. The combination of complete protein and magnesium makes quinoa particularly valuable for seniors who experience muscle cramps or feel weak during physical activity. The magnesium helps your muscles contract and relax smoothly, while the protein provides the building blocks for stronger muscle fibers. Quinoa also provides iron and B vitamins, both of which are crucial for energy production and muscle function. The iron helps deliver oxygen to your muscles during activity, while B vitamins help your body convert food into energy that your muscles can use. The versatility of quinoa makes it easy to include in multiple meals throughout the day. Use it as a breakfast porridge with milk and fruit, as a base for lunch salads, or as a side dish with your protein source at dinner. The key is to rinse quinoa thoroughly before cooking to remove its natural coating, which can taste bitter. For maximum muscle building benefit, combine quinoa with one of our previous protein sources. Try quinoa with scrambled eggs for breakfast or serve it alongside salmon for dinner. Continuing our journey through plant-based muscle builders, lentils and their legume cousins, chickpeas, black beans, and kidney beans represent one of the most underestimated muscle building food groups available to seniors. One cup of cooked lentils provides 18 grams of protein along with a remarkable array of nutrients that support muscle health in ways that might surprise you. While legumes aren't complete proteins on their own, they're incredibly rich in lysine, an amino acid that's often lacking in grains. This is why traditional food combinations like rice and beans or hummus with pita bread are so nutritionally intelligent. They create complete proteins that rival those found in animal foods. Lentils are particularly rich in folate, a B vitamin that's essential for cell division and protein synthesis. As you age, your body's ability to absorb folate decreases. Yet, your need for it increases because folate is required for building new muscle cells. The folate in lentils helps ensure that your body can actually create new muscle tissue from the protein you're eating. The iron content in lentils is substantial, providing about 37% of your daily needs in just one cup. While this is non-heem iron that's not as easily absorbed as the iron in meat, you can dramatically improve absorption by eating lentils with vitamin C rich foods like tomatoes, bell peppers, or citrus fruits. Legumes also provide potassium, which is crucial for proper muscle function and preventing cramps. Many seniors don't get enough potassium, and this deficiency can contribute to muscle weakness and fatigue. The potassium in legumes helps your muscles contract efficiently and recover properly after activity. The fiber in legumes provides an additional benefit that's often overlooked in muscle building discussions. Fiber helps regulate blood sugar levels, preventing the spikes and crashes that can interfere with muscle building. Stable blood sugar also helps optimize hormone production, including growth hormone and insulin-like growth factor, both of which are important for muscle development. One of the greatest advantages of legumes is their convenience and affordability. Dried lentils cook quickly without requiring soaking, making them perfect for busy seniors. Canned legumes are equally nutritious and even more convenient. Just rinse them to reduce sodium content. From the substantial protein of legumes, we move to some of nature's most concentrated muscle building packages, nuts and seeds. These small but mighty foods pack an incredible nutritional punch that can significantly enhance your muscle building efforts. What makes nuts and seeds particularly valuable is their unique combination of protein, healthy fats, and essential minerals that work together to optimize muscle function. Almonds lead the way with 6 g of protein per ounce along with vitamin E, magnesium, and healthy monounsaturated fats. Vitamin E is a powerful antioxidant that protects your muscle cells from damage during exercise and recovery. As you age, your muscles become more susceptible to oxidative damage, making the antioxidant protection from almonds increasingly important. Pumpkin seeds are absolute powerhouses for muscle building, providing 9 g of protein per ounce, along with exceptional amounts of zinc and magnesium. The zinc in pumpkin seeds is particularly important for seniors because zinc deficiency becomes more common with age and can significantly impair muscle building and testosterone production. Just 1 oz of pumpkin seeds provides about 23% of your daily zinc needs. Chia seeds and flax seeds bring something special to your muscle building arsenal. Plant-based omega-3 fatty acids and complete proteins. 2 tablespoons of chia seeds contain 4 g of complete protein and provide all essential amino acids. These tiny seeds also expand when mixed with liquid, helping you feel full and satisfied while providing sustained energy. Walnuts are unique among nuts because they're exceptionally rich in alpha lolenic acid, a plant-based omega-3 fatty acid. While not as potent as the omega-3s found in fish, the ALA in walnuts still helps reduce inflammation and support muscle recovery. The combination of protein, healthy fats, and omega-3s makes walnuts particularly beneficial for seniors dealing with joint pain or muscle inflammation. The healthy fats in nuts and seeds serve multiple purposes for muscle building. They help reduce inflammation, support hormone production, and improve the absorption of fats soluble vitamins like vitamin D. These fats also provide concentrated energy that can fuel your workouts and daily activities. The key with nuts and seeds is understanding proper portion sizes. While they're incredibly nutritious, they're also calorie dense. A serving is typically 1 oz or about a small handful. This amount provides significant nutritional benefits without excessive calories. Try adding a variety of nuts and seeds to your daily routine. Sprinkle them on Greek yogurt, add them to salads, or enjoy a small handful as a between meal snack. Soaking nuts overnight can make them easier to digest if you have sensitive digestion. While nuts and seeds provide concentrated nutrition, our ninth muscle building food serves a different but equally important role in rebuilding your muscle mass. Sweet potatoes. Although sweet potatoes aren't high in protein themselves, they're absolutely essential for maximizing the muscle building potential of all the protein richch foods we've discussed. Here's why sweet potatoes are crucial for your muscles. They provide the high quality carbohydrates that your muscles need for energy. When you don't eat enough carbohydrates, your body starts breaking down the protein from your meals and using it for energy instead of muscle building. Sweet potatoes prevent this from happening by providing your body with its preferred energy source. One medium sweet potato provides sustained energy through complex carbohydrates that are absorbed gradually, preventing the blood sugar spikes and crashes that can interfere with muscle building. This steady energy supply helps optimize hormone production, including insulin, which plays a crucial role in driving amino acids into your muscle cells. Sweet potatoes are incredibly rich in beta carotene, which your body converts to vitamin A. Vitamin A is essential for protein synthesis and muscle growth. Yet, many seniors don't get adequate amounts. The beta carotene in sweet potatoes is particularly well absorbed when the potatoes are cooked with a small amount of fat, making them perfect paired with nuts or olive oil. The potassium content in sweet potatoes is substantial, providing more potassium per serving than bananas. This potassium helps prevent muscle cramps and supports proper muscle function during physical activity. Many seniors experience muscle cramps due to potassium deficiency, making sweet potatoes an excellent addition to a muscle building diet. Sweet potatoes also provide vitamin C which enhances iron absorption from plant-based sources and supports collagen synthesis. Collagen is important for maintaining the structural integrity of your muscles and connective tissues. The fiber in sweet potatoes helps slow the absorption of sugars. Maintaining stable blood sugar levels that support optimal muscle building. This fiber also promotes digestive health which is important for absorbing all the nutrients from your muscle building foods. Try baking sweet potatoes and topping them with Greek yogurt and nuts for a perfect post-workout meal. Sweet potato fries baked in the oven with a light coating of olive oil make an excellent side dish, and mashed sweet potatoes pair beautifully with any of our protein sources. Our final muscle building champion brings us full circle to one of nature's most perfectly designed muscle building beverages, milk. Whether you choose dairy milk or fortified plant-based alternatives, milk can be an exceptional addition to your muscle building arsenal, providing a unique combination of nutrients that work together in powerful ways. One cup of whole milk provides 8 g of complete protein. But what makes milk truly special is that it contains both whey and casein proteins in the ideal ratio. This means you get the immediate muscle building benefits of fast absorbing whey protein combined with the sustained amino acid release of slow digesting casein protein. This dual protein system is exactly what your aging muscles need to stay in building mode throughout the day. The whey protein in milk is particularly rich in leucine, the amino acid that acts as a trigger for muscle protein synthesis. Research has shown that the leucine content in milk can help overcome the age- related resistance to muscle building that many seniors experience. This makes milk especially valuable for older adults who need a stronger signal to start the muscle building process. Milk is also an excellent source of calcium and vitamin D, both of which are crucial for muscle function and often deficient in seniors. The calcium in milk is particularly well absorbed because it's paired with protein and phosphorus, creating an optimal environment for absorption. This is important because calcium absorption decreases significantly with age. The natural sugars in milk, primarily lactose, provide quick energy that can help fuel your workouts and support recovery. The combination of protein and carbohydrates in milk creates an ideal environment for muscle protein synthesis, making it an excellent post exercise beverage. Recent research has shown that drinking milk after resistance exercise can enhance muscle protein synthesis and promote muscle growth in older adults more effectively than many commercial sports drinks. The natural combination of nutrients in milk provides everything your muscles need for optimal recovery and growth. If you're lactose intolerant, don't worry. Lactose-free milk provides the same nutritional benefits without the digestive discomfort. Plant-based alternatives like soy milk, pea protein milk, or fortified almond milk can also be excellent choices. Just ensure they're fortified with calcium and vitamin D. For maximum muscle building benefit, try having a glass of milk with your post-workout meal. Use milk as the base for protein smoothies with fruits and nuts. or enjoy a warm glass of milk before bed to provide your muscles with overnight rebuilding materials. The beauty of milk is its convenience and versatility. It requires no preparation, travels well, and can be easily incorporated into meals and snacks throughout the day. Now that you understand the individual power of each of these 10 muscle building foods, the real magic happens when you learn to combine them strategically throughout your day. The key to fighting sarcopenia isn't just eating these foods individually. It's about creating a symphony of nutrients that work together to keep your muscles in constant rebuilding mode. Start your day with a protein richch breakfast that sets the tone for muscle building. Try scrambled eggs with a side of Greek yogurt topped with berries and crushed walnuts. This combination provides both fast and slow digesting proteins, antioxidants to reduce inflammation, and healthy fats to support hormone production. For lunch, consider a quinoa salad loaded with chickpeas, almonds, and pieces of grilled salmon. This meal provides complete proteins from multiple sources, complex carbohydrates for sustained energy, and omega-3 fatty acids for inflammation control. The variety of amino acid profiles ensures your muscles receive everything they need for optimal growth. Your afternoon snack becomes a strategic muscle feeding opportunity. Try cottage cheese mixed with chopped nuts and a small amount of fruit. Or create a smoothie using milk, Greek yogurt, and a handful of berries. These snacks help maintain steady amino acid levels in your blood, preventing muscle breakdown between meals. For dinner, combine lean beef with roasted sweet potatoes and a side of lentils. This combination provides complete proteins, energizing carbohydrates, and fiber to support digestion and blood sugar control. The sweet potatoes provide the energy your muscles need, while the beef and lentils deliver complimentary amino acid profiles. The timing of your protein intake is crucial for maximizing muscle building. Research shows that seniors should aim for 20 to 30 g of protein at each meal rather than having most of their protein at dinner. This approach helps optimize muscle protein synthesis throughout the day and prevents the muscle breakdown that can occur during long periods without adequate protein. Consider adding a bedtime snack of cottage cheese or a glass of milk. This provides your muscles with slow digesting proteins that prevent muscle breakdown during the overnight fast. Many seniors unknowingly lose muscle mass during sleep because they don't have adequate amino acids Beyond strategically combining these foods, several additional factors can dramatically enhance their muscle building effectiveness. First, and most importantly, stay consistently hydrated. Water is essential for protein synthesis and helps transport nutrients to your muscles. Dehydration can reduce muscle protein synthesis by up to 20%. Making adequate fluid intake crucial for muscle building. Consider the timing of your meals around physical activity. Eating a combination of protein and carbohydrates within two hours after exercise can help maximize muscle protein synthesis and recovery. This doesn't mean you need to eat immediately after exercise, but don't wait too long. The post exercise period is when your muscles are most receptive to building new tissue. Pay attention to your vitamin D status. Many seniors are severely deficient in this crucial nutrient, which can significantly impact muscle strength and function. While fatty fish and fortified milk provide some vitamin D, you may need supplements to reach optimal levels. Consult with your health care provider about testing your vitamin D levels and determining if supplementation is necessary. Cooking methods can significantly impact the nutritional value of your foods. Gentle cooking methods like steaming, poaching, and slow cooking help preserve protein quality while making foods easier to digest. Avoid overcooking proteins as excessive heat can reduce their biological value and make them less effective for muscle building. Consider your individual needs and any challenges you face with eating. If you have difficulty chewing or swallowing, focus on softer proteins like eggs, yogurt, cottage cheese, and ground meats. If you're vegetarian or vegan, make sure to combine different plant proteins throughout the day to ensure you're getting all essential amino acids. Don't underestimate the importance of consistency. Building muscle is a gradual process that requires consistent nutrition over weeks and months. You won't see dramatic changes overnight, but with persistent effort, you will notice improvements in your strength, energy, and overall vitality. Remember that these foods work best as part of an overall healthy lifestyle that includes regular physical activity, adequate sleep, and stress management. The nutrients in these foods provide the raw materials for muscle building, but your muscles need the stimulus of physical activity to actually grow stronger. As we conclude this comprehensive guide to the 10 most powerful foods for stopping sarcopenia and rebuilding muscle mass, I want you to understand that you now possess knowledge that can literally transform your physical capabilities and independence as you age. Sarcopenia might be common among seniors, but it's absolutely not inevitable when you know how to fight back with the right nutrition. These 10 foods: eggs, Greek yogurt, salmon and fatty fish, lean beef, cottage cheese, quinoa, lentils and legumes, nuts and seeds, sweet potatoes, and milk represent your arsenal in the battle against muscle loss. They're not expensive supplements with questionable benefits or hard to find exotic ingredients. They're real whole foods that have been supporting human health for generations and are available in every grocery store. The key to success is starting where you are and building gradually. You don't need to completely overhaul your diet overnight. Begin by incorporating one or two of these foods into your daily routine this week. Maybe start your day with eggs and Greek yogurt, or add a serving of salmon to your weekly meal plan. Small, consistent changes will compound into dramatic improvements over time. I encourage you to share this information with friends and family members who could benefit from it. Muscle loss affects millions of seniors, but with the right knowledge, we can help more people maintain their strength and independence as they age. If this information has been valuable to you, please give this video a thumbs up and subscribe to our channel for more evidence-based health and nutrition content designed specifically for seniors. Your support helps us continue providing this crucial information to people who need it

I was nauseous and couldn’t eat for about 6 weeks lost another 35 lbs which put me at 50% of my beginning weight at my one year surgery appointment in Aug 2008.  I was so weak I couldn’t open a screw top bottle, and many doors. I was unable to be on my feet for more than about 10 mins, if that. 
Never seemed to be able to get all of my strength back, but I did get to functioning and working my 12 hr nursing shifts at the hospital. Exhaustion followed me. Didn’t have a normal appetite and didn’t eat much but gained some weight, about 15-20 lbs, then lost that again and was barely eating. I have absolutely no craving for carbs or sweets which had always been a problem for me on my previous lower protein diet. My chest has gone up .25”, I haven’t been measuring my thighs but they feel tighter in my pants and firmer on top. I have been able to slowly increase what I am lifting at the gym. So I don’t know if sarcopenia is reversible, but I am trying. I have been taking creatine daily and l-citrulline on workouts days. Need to add the leucine, it sounds like.
I hadn’t heard much talk about sarcopenia but thought to myself how I would see people at the hospital who seemed weak for no reason other than inactivity. I don’t want that to be me. My daughter had a neuromuscular disease that left her unable to move and took her life.  She would have given anything to be able to walk. Health is precious, we have to work our bodies to keep them going as long as we can!

but at what age do we start losing muscle the surprising answer to this is after the age of 30 after the age of 30 you begin to lose as much as 3 to 5% of your muscle per decade and most men will lose about 30% of their muscle mass during their lifetime women lose slightly less the positive news is that you can avoid delay and even reverse this muscle loss in old age and here are four evidence-based strategies to keep you strong get you stronger reduce your Fall's risk reduce your risk of fractures and maintain your Independence and quality of life the first strategy is to increase your protein intake older men often experience a phenomenon known as anabolic resistance which lowers the body's ability to break down and synthesize protein a recent study in the journal nutrients suggests a daily intake of 1 to 1.3 g of protein per kilogram of body weight for older adults who do resistance training for example a 200b man would need about 90 to 118 g of protein per day the following foods are your classic sources of protein meat fish eggs milk beans lentils cottage cheese and tofu are some examples the next strategy is PRT progressive resistance training resistance training will help you to hold onto your muscle or even increase it as researchers in this review found out with a total of 1,328 participants male and female over 49 different studies and their age ranges between 50 and 83 the subjects averaged a 2.4 pound increase in lean muscle mass so here's what you need to do 8 to 10 exercises that Target all the major muscle groups sets of 12 to 15 repetitions performed at an effort of about 5 to seven on a 10 point scale and you only need to do two to three workouts per week if you were to find this plan too easy then you can increase the intensity and the effort that you put into each session now the third strategy is a simple daily walk it's no secret that walking is good for your health the simple routine of a daily brisk walk for 30 minutes or more is one of the best health Investments you can make walking has been shown to reduce back pain improve balance and help our brain and mental health the final strategy is sleep having - Are You Eating Enough Protein for Your Age & Goals? Women’s Optimal Protein Intake Explained: Kind: captions Language: en Let's bring protein into the conversation because in the past decades we've heard a lot about carbohydrate, low carb, high carb, fat, low fat, highfat, but protein's kind of been out of the conversation until recently. There's a lot of confusion right now about what is optimal for protein. We're hearing people push on the upwards of 3 1/2 g per kilogram, which is about 1.8 g per pound. We also hear people say no 50 gram of protein total per day for women is adequate. It's actually somewhere in between because if we look at the recommended daily allowance right now we see it's around8 kilo g of body weight. And that I want people to understand is the bare minimum that you need to consume to prevent malnutrition. When we're looking at an active individual as well as an active aging individual, we need more. When we bring our sex differences into effect, we see that women age differently than men in the fact that we have perry and postmenopause. Men age in a linear fashion. Women have a really specific point in their lives where all of a sudden their bodies responding differently. Why I'm bringing that up is because as we get older, our bodies, both men and women, become more resistant to the growth effects of protein and growth effects of exercise. So we need to look at dosing more of both. So for women as we get into our 40s onward, we are looking more at that 1 g to 1.2 g of protein per pound of body weight, which sits around that 2 to 2.2 g per kilo. When we're looking at women who are in their reproductive years, 18 to about 35 to 40, 1.6 six gram per kilogram or hitting about that.9 g per pound is the bare minimum that you want to have if you're an active individual. Of course, in times where you're trying to build through strength training, you're going to increase your protein. So, when we're looking at protein and protein effects, it's not just about building muscle and maintaining lean mass. There is a critical component of the amino acids of protein that go to help with bone and bone density. We also see amino acids are important for neurotransmitters, for nerve conduction, for so many other functions within the body. So, if we're low in protein, then we're going to have systemic effects that we might not notice right away, but down the line, there's definitely an impact, especially when it comes to brain, bone, and muscle health. I think two of the biggest things to take away from protein and the intake capacity is we've neglected it for so long and it's really difficult for so many women to wrap their heads around increasing protein and protein needs but the longer term effect of maintaining just that RDA is the face that we have of osteopenia cognitive decline cycopenia so if we start earlier by increasing our total protein intake and managing that intake across the day. We're going to have a stronger skeletal system, a stronger muscularkeeletal system, and a better brain health. If we start looking at increasing our protein intake from that8 g per pound, trying to eat our way up to at least 1 g per pound, but ideally 1.2 to 1.3 g per pound. Because lifestyle and life phase can affect how much protein you need from puberty, pregnancy, reproductive years, pmenopause, post-menopause. It's hard to condense it all into one short YouTube segment. I will refer you to the position stand we wrote with the International Society of Sport Nutrition last year on the female athlete that actually encompasses all the lifespan and how much protein you need across the day as well as pre and post exercise. So many people think that getting that one gram per pound of body weight in protein is really difficult. But if we pay attention, it's really not that difficult. We can look at how do we get 30 to 40 g at each meal, maybe 15 to 20 at each snack, and what the quality of that protein is. So if you're feeling overwhelmed with increasing protein, make small steps over the next 3 weeks. Increase your protein from what you're having now to trying to hit that 1 g per pound. is very achievable, but it does take some steps to get used to thinking about protein and putting it on your plate. So, if you have any questions, shoot them in the comments below. Thanks for watching.

- Avoid Muscle Loss After 40 | Protein Diet, Yoga & Strength Tips | Dr Hansaji: Kind: captions Language: en Namaskar. Did you know that after the age of 30, adults lose 3 to 5% of their muscle mass every decade, and after 40, this loss speeds dramatically. The surprising part is it's not just about looking less toned. It directly affects your strength, balance, metabolism, and even how long you live. The real mistake most people make after 40 thinking that just eating less or only walking every day is enough to stay fit. The truth is that if you're not actively protecting your muscles, you are slowly losing them, and with it your energy, your vitality, and your independence. But here's the good news. With the right food, the right type of exercise, and little smart recovery, you can actually stop muscle loss and stay strong into your 50s, 60s, and beyond. The first and most important step is to give your muscles the building block that they need is the high-quality protein. At this age, your body doesn't absorb protein as efficiently as it used to, which means that you need to be more mindful of your diet. If your meals are only rice, roti, vegetables without enough protein, your muscles will keep breaking down. What you should do is include natural protein-rich food in every meal. Moong dal, paneer, tofu, curd, nuts and seeds, all these help rebuild and repair muscle tissue. Even something simple like taking a handful of roasted chana or pumpkin seed as a snack makes difference. One simple recipe you can make is moong dal chilla stuffed with paneer and spinach. Moong dal is light, easy to digest, and packed with plant protein. Paneer adds calcium and slow digestive protein, while spinach gives iron and magnesium to support muscle function. Just soak moong dal overnight, grind it into a batter, spread it like dosa, and stuff it with clean sauteed paneer and spinach. This way you're giving your muscle a complete nourishment in a form that is tasty, easy to make, and filling even on busy morning. And if you prefer something quick to drink, you can try a banana almond protein smoothie. Blend one ripe banana, 8 to 10 soaked almonds, half a cup of Greek yogurt or soy milk, and a spoon of chia seed. This gives you potassium, magnesium, healthy fats, and good protein in one glass. A perfect way to begin the day without feeling heavy, yet keeping your muscles strong. And next thing you must not ignore is doing some weight-bearing exercises or doing some asanas. Many people over 40 thinks exercise should only means walking or light yoga. Those are wonderful, but if you completely skip weight-bearing movements, your muscles won't get signals to grow and stay strong. Remember, muscles follow the principle of use it or lose it. You don't have to lift heavy weight in gym to see the result. Even simple bodyweight asanas done at home like Utkatasana, Adho Mukha Svanasana are enough to activate the major muscle group. Just 15-20 minutes three to four times in a week will make a huge difference. When you give your body this kind of resistance, the protein you eat is directed in building muscles rather than getting stored as fat. These weight-bearing practices also do something deeper. It keeps your bones strong, improve balance, and even helps regulate hormones like testosterone and growth hormone, both of which naturally decline after 40. This is why those who combine good protein intake with regular strength training look and feel much younger than their age. And then, don't forget recovery. Many people think more activity is always better, but if you don't rest well, your muscles don't repair, and you feel constant fatigue. Prioritize good sleep, stay hydrated, and include food rich in magnesium like green leafy vegetables and seeds. These help your muscles relax, prevent cramps, and make sure you wake up with energy instead of stiffness. You see, muscle loss after 40 is not inevitable. If you nourish your body with the right protein, engage your muscles with resistance practices, and give them time to recover, you can maintain your vitality, independence, and confidence into later years. So don't make a mistake of neglecting your muscles. Protect them, and they will protect you.
