- From Drained To Disciplined - The Routine That Changed Everything | Weekly Fitness Vlog: Kind: captions Language: en Every Monday I swore I would start fresh and by Wednesday I was already burnt out. The truth is motivation never saved me. But what did a romanticized morning ritual so simple it almost feels silly. But it turned my chaotic mornings into the calst part of my day. And once I saw that, I realized that discipline isn't about trying. It's not about trying hard at all. It's about building something you cannot fail at. A couple years ago, I was in a really dark place. Depression had me in a deep hole mentally, emotionally, and physically. Dishes would pile up and my skin definitely reflected how little I cared about myself. And the weight of even the simple tasks felt unbearable. I stopped seeing the point of trying. And the deeper I let things slide, the deeper I fell into that hole. And even now, I still have moments where those feelings would just creep back in. But the difference now is that I'm in a healthier state of mind. And I have the small habits that I've built that helps me fight back. Discipline became less about fixing myself and more about just caring about myself. Even on the days I just don't feel like it. The shift happened when I stopped waiting for motivation. Because motivation is temporary. It shows up when you feel good, when life feels light. But what about the days it doesn't? When you feel low and motivation isn't there. And that's when I realized motivation isn't about forcing yourself. It's about creating little anchors that just keep you steady no matter how you feel. For me, that started with my mornings. I wanted to romanticize them. I wanted them to feel romanticized. I wanted to feel calm and have my mornings be more intentional because the first choice that you make each day sets the tone for everything else that follows. Now, instead of reaching for my phone the second I wake up, I give myself space. I make coffee. I spend time with God. I sit in the quiet. Sometimes I journal. Sometimes I just sit there, breathe, and let my thoughts land on paper. It's not about being productive in the moment. It's about starting the day with peace instead of chaos. Avoiding social media first thing in the morning changed everything for me. Before my mornings were hijacked by other people's lives, comparison, distraction, stress. Now those first few minutes belong to me. And because of that, the rest of my day feel more grounded, like I'm actually leading instead of reacting. The other shift was learning to rely on systems, not moods. If I waited to feel like it, working out or eating well, or even doing my skin care, I'd be stuck. But if I set up systems, laying out gym clothes the night before, meal prepping so I don't have to decide what to eat, scheduling my workouts like appointments. Discipline became automatic. It wasn't about being superhuman. It was about removing as many decisions as possible so I can just just show up. The funny thing is once I stopped chasing motivation and started trusting my system, I actually felt more free. My days stopped being constant mental negotiations of should I, shouldn't I, and became simple rhythms that made me feel proud of myself. Here's the part I think most people get wrong about discipline. It's not about perfection. You're going to slip. You're going to have days where you skip the gym or just eat the things you should not eat. That doesn't erase discipline. What matters is how fast you reset. For me, the most disciplined thing I ever learned was forgiving myself and doing that quickly and just moving forward. That mindset saved me because discipline isn't about being flawless. It's about being resilient. And the deeper I got into this, the more I realized discipline is tied to identity. When I thought of myself as someone trying to be disciplined, it always felt fragile. But when I started saying I am disciplined, I am a disciplined woman, it became a part of me. every small choice, making the bed, putting on moisturizer after of course doing my skin care, cooking instead of ordering out was proof of that identity. And that proof that proof built trust within myself. So if you're in a place where discipline just feels impossible, I want you to know this. It doesn't start with motivation and it does not start with changing everything overnight because that's just doesn't make sense. It starts with the tiniest choices that you repeat until they become who you are. For me, that meant protecting my mornings, building systems, forgiving myself fast, and anchoring discipline to selfrespect instead of punishment. And little by little that turned into peace, balance, and a version of myself I can be so proud of. If this resonates with you, you are in the right place. This is the kind of honest conversations I'll be having here. So, if you're ready to build your own version of discipline, calm, sustainable, and rooted in selfrespect, join me, cuz we're going to do this together. >> So, don't you dare feel alone. The hard part is over. Waking up early. We nourished our mind, our spirit, our body with that amazing breakfast. And now it's time to go to the gym and show up for ourself. In the gym, that is where the discipline really shows. That is where you get to practice it even more. When I'm training in the gym, I like to remind myself that my goal is not perfection. My goal is to practice consistency and become more consistent. You know, because training in the gym is hard. Training is very hard. - Mindful 40+ Diaries 🍂Calm and Cozy Morning At Home | Seasonal Baking and Cooking | SILENT VLOG: Kind: captions Language: en-US Good morning, friends! I hope your week’s been going well so far. This morning, I felt like making waffles. To make them a bit healthier, I swap regular flour for oat flour. I don’t usually buy oat flour. If I need some, I just blend rolled oats in my coffee grinder until they’re nice and fine. In a bowl, whisk together oat flour, baking powder, baking soda, salt, and cinnamon. I’ll leave the recipe in the description box below the video. In another bowl, whisk together all the wet ingredients. It’s full-on autumn outside, so of course, they had to have pumpkin puree. I make it myself, just roast the pumpkin in the oven and blend it until smooth. Pour the wet ingredients into the dry and stir gently until just combined. The batter should be thick but easy to spoon. Add a splash of milk or water if it’s too thick. As the waffles cook, I get my coffee ready. My Meraki espresso machine has already become part of my routine. The machine measures everything by weight, so it’s easy to stay consistent. Meaning each shot has the perfect strength, flavor, and balance, and I can experiment with different doses without guesswork. I love that everything I need is included: the grinder, the scales, and all the accessories. It really feels like having my own little coffee bar in my kitchen. It’s quiet, durable, and the touchscreen is so easy to use. Cleaning is simple with removable parts, and I love that I can track everything with the integrated timers and scales. Every espresso I pull comes out balanced, rich, and with that beautiful crema. I absolutely love the steam wand, it whips up silky foam in seconds. I think that’s what I love most about the Meraki, it makes great coffee feel effortless, but it still lets you enjoy the craft behind it. Waffles on the plate, coffee in hand, and a few peaceful minutes to myself. After breakfast, I load the dirty dishes into the dishwasher, wipe down the counters and put everything back in place. Doing this keeps the kitchen clean and fresh, and makes it easier to start the next meal. A few simple steps, and the space feels calm, organized, and ready for the day ahead. For a quick and gentle clean, I just mix a cup of warm water with a drop or two of dish soap. It cleans gently and leaves the kitchen feeling even fresher. The laundry I loaded early in the morning has already been washed and dried. Now it’s time to iron it and put everything away neatly in the dresser. I used to use a big ironing board and a heavy iron, which took forever. Now it’s so much quicker and easier with my small, super handy ironing board and steamer. Choosing the right tools makes everyday tasks faster, easier, and even enjoyable. When I try to fold a pillowcase the usual way, it falls apart. But if I fold it like this, it holds its shape, and my drawers stay neat for longer. Everything stays neat and in place, even when I take something from the middle. Folding a huge fitted sheet neatly and compactly is quite a challenge. I do it like this: first, I hold the sheet by the corners, tuck them into each other, then lay it on a flat surface, smooth it out, and fold it step by step until it’s a neat, compact rectangle. The other day, I was at the Homegoods and spotted this set of three kitchen towels on sale—60% off! I couldn’t resist getting them. They’re a neutral shade with a lovely autumn-inspired design. Autumn always makes me want to bake something delicious that fills the house with a wonderful smell. Recently, I found a healthy pie recipe that’s mostly apples and barely any dough, so it’s lighter on the waistline. I started with the apples. You can peel them if you like, I decided to leave the skin on. Then, remove the cores and cut them into thin slices. I have four medium-sized apples. If yours are large, you can use three. The apples should be sliced very thinly. Preheat the oven to 360°F (180°C). In a bowl, combine two eggs, 2 tbsp of sugar (or adjust to your taste), 40 g of melted coconut butter (a bit less than 1/4 cup) , 1 tbsp vanilla extract, 4–6 tbsp of flour, 1/2 tsp of baking powder, and a pinch of salt. The batter should be slightly runny. Pour it over the sliced apples and gently mix until all the pieces are nicely coated with the batter. Grease the bottom of the pie dish with butter and lightly dust it with flour. Fill it with the apple and batter mixture, then gently press down with a spatula. Sprinkle some cinnamon for a lovely aroma and add a few almond flakes on top. Bake in a preheated oven for about an hour, until the top turns a nice light golden color. Allow it to cool completely before cutting into slices. I love browsing home décor stores, not just at the start of a new season, but also closer to the end of the season. That’s when you can often find really good deals on beautiful items. I enjoy checking out cozy seasonal colors and sometimes even spotting items I didn’t expect to find on sale. It’s a little treasure hunt every time. Finding something I love is great, but finding it on sale makes it feel twice as rewarding. Stores haven’t rolled out the biggest discounts yet, but Christmas products are starting to show up. For lunch, I want to make a flavorful salmon soup, it’s actually one of my favorites. In a large pot over medium heat, cook onion, garlic, and carrots for 5 minutes until softened. Stir in diced potatoes, bay leaf, and water (or broth). Bring to a boil, then reduce heat and simmer 10 minutes until potatoes are tender. Cut salmon into bite-sized cubes. Set aside. Place the salmon pieces into the simmering soup and cook for 5–7 minutes until tender. Add dill, salt, and pepper to taste, then stir in 1/2 cup (120ml) of half-and-half or heavy cream. Heat gently for 1–2 minutes without boiling. Time for afternoon tea, a little break before I move on with the rest of the day. My favorite moment to slow down and enjoy something warm and comforting. The pie came out so soft and light, I really recommend giving it a try. Thank you for joining me this morning. Wishing you a nice and peaceful week ahead. Take care, and I’ll see you in the next video.

Ovarian Problems
1. Polycystic Ovary Syndrome (PCOS) – A hormonal disorder causing irregular periods, ovarian cysts, and infertility.
2. Ovarian Cysts – Fluid-filled sacs in the ovaries that may cause pain or go unnoticed.
3. Ovarian Cancer – A serious condition that can develop in the ovaries, often with vague symptoms.
4. Ovarian Torsion – A twisting of the ovary, cutting off blood supply and causing severe pain.
5. Premature Ovarian Insufficiency (POI) – When ovaries stop functioning normally before the age of 40, leading to early menopause.
Uterine Problems
1. Fibroids – Noncancerous growths in the uterus that may cause heavy periods and pain.
2. Endometriosis – A condition where tissue similar to the uterine lining grows outside the uterus, leading to pain and fertility issues.
3. Adenomyosis – The uterine lining grows into the muscle wall, causing heavy, painful periods.
4. Uterine Prolapse – Weak pelvic muscles lead to the uterus slipping down into the vaginal canal.
5. Uterine Cancer – Includes endometrial cancer, which affects the lining of the uterus.

I will love to hear from you.
Recently I got Certification of Nutritionist (just completed the course)

I work with women all over the world to help them understand the connection between food and hormones so they can feel confident and in control of their health. So, let's start with the most common question that I get asked about keto and periods. Can keto make your period disappear? Well, yes, it can. Especially if your overall energy intake drops too low. When you don't eat enough calories to support your basic metabolic needs, plus exercise and normal hormone function, your brain can reduce the signals that trigger ovulation. This is known as hypothalamic amenorhea and it can cause your period to become irregular or stop altogether. It's a red flag that your body isn't feeling safe enough to prioritize reproduction. Now, that said, not everyone on keto loses their period. It really comes down to how restrictive your diet becomes and whether you're meeting your individual nutritional needs. Now, you might also notice changes in flow. Some women report lighter periods after switching to keto, likely because of the reduced estrogen levels that can come with lower body fat or calorie restriction. Others might see heavier or more painful periods, particularly if the keto diet is causing inflammation, stress, or affecting thyroid function. And if you've come off the pill and started keto at the same time, it can be hard to know what's causing what. Cycle length can also change. Some women find their cycles get longer or more irregular, especially if you're not ovulating consistently. Others notice that their premenstrual symptoms, such as bloating or mood swings, improve when they remove high GI carbohydrates and reduce sugar intake. So, how do you know if keto is helping or harming your cycle? Well, I encourage you to track your cycle. Note the length, flow, symptoms, and any changes in how you're feeling emotionally and physically. This can help you to identify trends and have more meaningful conversations with your healthare team. Now, I'm not here to say whether keto is good or bad. In fact, some women, especially those with insulin resistance or peacos, it can help them to regulate cycles in the short term. But if your calorie intake becomes so low that you're not ovulating, it can impact your fertility and health in the long term. The right diet is different for everybody. So, it's definitely not a one-sizefits-all approach. So, what would I recommend if you do see significant changes to your menstrual cycle whilst on keto? Well, firstly, make sure that you work with a dietitian to ensure that you're still meeting your nutritional needs, including adequate calories, fiber, and essential micronutrients. You can book a nutrition assessment with our team using the link in the description box. Then, secondly, check in with your GP or fertility specialist, especially if your period has stopped or become very irregular. And remember that just because a diet is popular doesn't mean that it's the right one for your body or your hormones. So, how does the keto diet affect your menstrual cycle? Well, it can lead to mis periods, changes in flow, or altered cycle length, especially if it causes energy restriction or weight loss. But it all comes down to how you implement it and how your body responds. I definitely wouldn't recommend using it as a contraceptive. If you've noticed any changes to your period after starting keto, I'd love to hear about your experience in the comments. And if this video has helped clarify things, please give it a like and subscribe to the channel and share it with a friend. Thanks for watching and I look forward to seeing you in my next video. So, um, and I might just you guys bit fast here. Thank you. I need Yep.

- Is the Keto Diet Good or Bad for Women? Gut Health & Recovery Nutrition | Dr. Stacy Sims: Kind: captions Language: en Do you think women should be eating immediately after they exercise and men or is it does it is there a variance there at all? There is a variance because when we look at um what we call metabolism coming back down to baseline. So that's your overall body coming back down to its resting state. For women it happens within 30 to 40 minutes after exercise. For men it's 2 to 18 hours depending on the intensity. So in that we see that if we want to maximize our body's resistance training and muscle building capacity, we need to give it some food. We need to give it some really good hit of protein. For women who are in their reproductive years, we see 35 grams of protein post exercise within 45 minutes will tip the muscle into muscle protein synthesis. For men, it's 20 grams and it can be 2, four, whatever hours later. When we're looking at returning our muscle glycogen back to normal, we don't need as much carbohydrate post exercise as a woman as men need more because they tap more into their stores. So the window of opportunity for women post exercise is around that 45 minute mark, but for men it's open a lot wider. What about the keto diet for women? I am kind of anti- keto for both sexes. And I say this because when we look at the gut microbiome that is so important, we see a decrease in diversity as we become more and more I guess city dwelling and we are having less and less of uh variety in our food chain. We have to take care of the gut microbiome. If we look at the ketogenic diet and the high fat intake that comes with it, it significantly decreases that gut microbiome diversity, which reduces the body's ability to synthesize vitamins, to produce serotonin, to have this conversation between the gut and the brain. And for women, we're already metabolically flexible by the nature of being born with more of those endurance fibers that there's no reason to try to do a ketogenic diet. Could I not take a prebiotic or something or just eat more fruits and veggies and stuff? So, if you're eating a lot of fruit and veggies, sorry, not fruit, veggies. No, if you're eating a lot of fibrous fruit and veg, then that's how we increase the diversity. Taking a probiotic pill, it just affects the upper intestines. But even that is a little bit suspect because there's only two to three companies that are making all the probiotics that are B2B. So that means business is business and we don't really know the long-term outcome and we can have the overgrowth of some probiotics that again can cause some dispiosis. Could I be on the keto diet and still protect my gut microbiome? I don't think so. Not from what I've seen. Cuz I thought the gut microbiome was predominantly about like plants. It is. But you also need some protein that comes from a wide variety of different sources. And the amount of fat that is taken in through a true ketogenic diet is 70 to 80% of your total intake coming from fat. And then that will cause the overgrowth of the bacteria that relies primarily on fatty acids, which downregulates all the good bacteria that relies on our fibrous fruit and veg. because you're not going to be able to consume as much fiber as you need on a ketogenic diet to really invoke this diversity. For thinking about invoking diversity, you want 30 different plants across the week. And on a ketogenic diet, you're just not capable of being able to eat as much to create that diversity. And the reason why it's really important for women to have that diversity is because we have some gut bugs that are responsible for our sex hormone metabolism. So, we think about estrogen, progesterone. People think, "Oh, yeah, well, it's released from the ovaries and the adrenals and it goes and it hits our target tissues." But we have this thing called a second pass where our sex hormones will be taken up by the liver, bound by sex hormone binding globbulin, shot into the intestines through bile, unconjugated or unpacked by these little gut bugs, and then shot back out in the circulation to work. If we have a lower diversity of the gut microbiome, we don't have those bugs that will help with our sex hormone. um I guess reactivation and the ability for the sex hormones to work optimally.

- Using the KETO diet to boost your TESTOSTERONE?!: Kind: captions Language: pt-BR Olá pessoal, vocês já se perguntaram como sua dieta afeta seus hormônios? Bem, hoje vou abordar como a dieta cetogênica afeta seus níveis de testosterona. Sou a Dra. Rena Malik, urologista e cirurgiã pélvica. Se você não sabia, o urologista é o médico do trato urinário genital. Cuidamos de problemas médicos, bem como de problemas cirúrgicos que afetam os rins, os órgãos urinários ou os tubos que drenam os rins, a bexiga e a uretra de onde sai a urina. e cuidamos dos problemas genitais. A testosterona é um hormônio masculino produzido pelos testículos e é muito importante para a produção de espermatozoides e também para a libido. Mas também faz mais do que isso. Também pode melhorar a massa muscular e reduzir a quantidade de gordura que você carrega consigo. Pode melhorar a densidade óssea e o bem-estar geral. A dieta cetogênica é uma dieta muito popular que normalmente é uma dieta com baixo teor de carboidratos e alto teor de gordura que tem sido muito popular recentemente para perda de peso, mas na verdade foi originalmente desenvolvida para pacientes com transtorno convulsivo refratário ou transtorno convulsivo que não podia ser controlado por medicamentos . E agora está em todo lugar. Você realmente não pode ir a lugar nenhum sem ouvir sobre a dieta cetônica ou ver algum lanche especial da dieta cetônica. O interessante da dieta cetogênica é que você reduz os carboidratos a um nível extremamente baixo em menos de 5% da sua ingestão diária. Também tem sido altamente elogiado por seus benefícios de perda de peso, bem como por suas propriedades de redução de insulina. E muito simplesmente, é essa falta de hidratos de carbono, que normalmente é a nossa fonte básica de combustível para todas as nossas atividades diárias, que quando diminui, o nosso corpo passa a utilizar gorduras para o nosso armazenamento de energia e ajuda-nos a perder essa gordura e a perder o excesso de peso. Quando o corpo usa essas gorduras, ele as decompõe em coisas chamadas corpos cetônicos. E esses corpos cetônicos podem ser facilmente usados pelo nosso cérebro e pelos nossos órgãos para criar energia. E é daí que vem o termo dieta cetogênica. Portanto, a testosterona é, na verdade, criada a partir de um precursor chamado colesterol. Porque a testosterona é criada a partir do colesterol. Quando você segue uma dieta rica em colesterol contendo itens ou muitos alimentos gordurosos, você tem mais matérias-primas necessárias para criar testosterona. E esse é o processo de pensamento pelo qual o uso de uma dieta cetogênica pode potencialmente aumentar seu nível de testosterona. Mas quais são os dados? Portanto, foi feito um estudo com 25 homens em idade universitária. Portanto, esses dados só seriam aplicáveis a outros homens em idade universitária se você os aplicasse a um público maior de pessoas. Todos esses homens participaram de um programa de treinamento de resistência. Exatamente o mesmo programa. E você tem que perceber que fazer qualquer tipo de treinamento intervalado de alta intensidade ou treinamento de resistência pode por si só aumentar sua testosterona. Mas eles fizeram exatamente o mesmo programa de treinamento. Eles os dividiram em dieta ocidental ou dieta cetogênica. E o que eles encontraram? Bem, eles mediram esses participantes durante 11 semanas e mediram a testosterona no início do estudo e mais tarde no final do estudo. O que eles descobriram foi que os estudantes da dieta cetogênica, estudantes em idade universitária, tinham um nível de testosterona mais alto em mais de 100 nanogramas por decilitro. E isso pode ser um pouco se você estiver no limite do baixo nível de testosterona. Mas não sei exatamente o que significaria uma diferença de 100 pontos se você estivesse na faixa normal. Existem duas maneiras principais pelas quais os participantes deste grupo poderiam ter aumentado a testosterona. Uma delas é que eles aumentaram a ingestão de colesterol. E sabemos que o grupo da dieta cetogênica consumiu mais colesterol do que o grupo não cetogênico ou o grupo da dieta ocidental. Mas não sabemos exatamente qual era a quantidade. Portanto, não podemos atribuir definitivamente esse aumento à testosterona. Mas a outra coisa que aconteceu no grupo da dieta cetogênica é que eles diminuíram a ingestão de fibras alimentares. E a fibra dietética realmente atua para ajudar a excretar ou remover mais testosterona do intestino. Então, acho que ainda não se sabe se a dieta cetogênica funciona aumentando o colesterol ou diminuindo a ingestão de fibras para melhorar seu nível geral de testosterona sérica. E, novamente, não sei o que a diferença de 100 nanogramas por decilitro realmente faz em alguém que tem um nível normal de testosterona. Então, qual é a minha lição de casa? Bem, eu realmente não sei se a dieta cetogênica é tão útil para aumentar a ingestão de testosterona, mas definitivamente foi demonstrado que aumenta a perda de peso e melhora os níveis de insulina em grandes porções da população. Então, se você quiser aprender mais sobre a dieta cetogênica, eu recomendaria assistir alguma literatura do Dr. Jason Fung ou ler alguns de seus livros, porque ele é um especialista nisso e realmente explicou bem como funciona a dieta cetogênica. Se você está curioso para saber como pode realmente aumentar seu nível de testosterona naturalmente, você pode conferir o vídeo que fiz sobre como aumentar sua testosterona naturalmente ou darei a você as quatro principais coisas que você pode fazer agora para ajudar a aumentar sua testosterona. Número um, você pode perder peso. Vários estudos demonstraram que a perda de peso aumenta os níveis de testosterona. Número dois, você pode aumentar a quantidade de testosterona se incluir exercícios em seu plano de perda de peso. E assim, o exercício que se mostrou útil é o treinamento intervalado de alta intensidade ou o treinamento de resistência, que também demonstrou aumentar o nível de testosterona. Número três, aumente o seu sono. Se você tiver um sono mais longo e reparador, foi demonstrado que isso também melhora seus níveis de testosterona. E número quatro, evite produtos químicos desreguladores endócrinos como o bisfenol A ou BPA, como aqueles encontrados em Tupperwares de plástico ou garrafas plásticas, que podem realmente interferir no seu sistema endócrino e tornar mais difícil para o seu corpo produzir a testosterona necessária. Portanto, troque essas garrafas plásticas de água por garrafas de vidro ou metal que não contenham plástico. Tudo bem, muito obrigado a vocês. So we go through creatine whenever we have fast energetics. So you think about the first 0 to 20 seconds of any kind of energy system within the body, brain, heart, muscle, gut. And we see that creatine monohydrate is so important for health, especially brain health. And we hear about it from a bodybuilding background where we have to use 5 g twice a day with carbohydrate to really boost the amount of creatine that's in the muscle. But that's not what I'm talking about. I'm talking about 3 to five grams daily. You can take a teaspoon and stir in your coffee because it's just a very easy powder that's really mixable. Doesn't taste like anything. Takes about three weeks to fully saturate. But we see from RCTs or randomized control trials for women that using creatine can help bring women out of an anxiety or a depressive state much faster than just using something like a serotonin reuptake inhibitor. We can look at Crea Vitalis website, creatine for health website. They're both one and the same. And it has a list of all the most recent publications on creatine for the health aspects, not just muscle performance. Collagen, I can't use collagen, but I wanted to bring it up. My husband uses it for joints and other structural aspects, but as a plant-based individual, I don't use collagen. There is a confusion between collagen protein and something like a normal protein powder. Collagen protein is not what we think of when we're using post exercise protein because it's a structural protein. It does not go into the quantification of your dietary protein. So if we're looking at collagen, there's two kinds. We have a native collagen and a peptide. A native collagen when ingested has an interaction with your immune system to reduce the inflammation and the signaling to degrade your cartilage. We see peptides are small bits of that whole collagen that are actually digested and those small bits of peptides go to the target tissue and are used to regenerate some of the cartilage but also to trigger a response to stop degrading the cartilage. So, it's a structural protein. If you want good hair, you want good skin, you want good nails, you want good joints, you're going for collagen. If you want muscle, muscle protein synthesis, nerve, bone, all of the things for dietary protein, you go for a protein supplement that is plant-based or whey isolate. The other must-haves really is an omega-3. It's really important for women who are super active. If we look at a cellular aspect of increasing our oxidative and antioxidative status, omega-3s are really essential for brain health, cellular health, and so it's one of my go-tos for sure. And then we get to adaptogens. These are two that I use on the regular. I use rodeiola and ashagandha. So I'll take a step back and tell you what an adaptogen is. An adaptogen is a plant compound that has an effect in the body that we don't normally see with a pharmaceutical. The best way I can explain it is if we look at seaweed and we see seaweed changes different colors. When it is in a dark cold part of the ocean, it has a higher protein content and it in itself is dark. When that same plant comes up closer to the surface in the summer months, it becomes lighter in color and has a reduced protein composition. The reason for that is it's adapting to the environment. So a plant in itself can adapt to different situations to different stressors. So there are certain plants that grow in really environmentally challenging conditions. So we can look at MKA, we can look at ashagandha, we can look at rodeiola. They adapt to the harsh environment that it's in. And because it can adapt, that same compound has a response to our human stress system. So if we look at something like rodeiola, it has multi-factoral effect on the body. We can see that it modulates estrogen for perry and post-menopausal women. So that means it either increases the sensitivity of your receptors or it decreases the sensitivity based on what your own individual body needs. It also has an effect on cortisol. It modulates your cortisol receptors as well. So, it helps with that tired but wired feeling by dropping cortisol's effect on the body so that you can get into more of a parasympathetic state. When I'm traveling with jet lag, I use it to help me get into a parasympathetic state for sleeping so that it can help my body reset its circadian rhythm and get me out of a jet lag state. With ashagandha, it's an overall stress reducer because it has a direct effect on cortisol cortisol responses, but it also has an effect on thyroid. So, it does have a contraindication for individuals who are using any kind of thyroid medication because again its adaptogenic response is it understands how stressed your body is from a metabolic as well as an autonomic nervous system. So, it can adjust your thyroid hormone sensitivity, but it also affects cortisol and how your body responds to cortisol. So, it's another one if you're going into a highly stressed situation or you're living a highly stressed life, it helps modulate that stress to reduce that overarching tired but wired feeling. So, that would be the cadre of supplements that I use on the regular. And when we're looking at all of our individual basis of when do we use supplements, they are actually that a supplement. We want to look at real food first, but sometimes there are holes in our diet where we need to fill and that's where some supplementation can come into play. Or it might be that we don't want to go onto a true westernized pharmaceutical so that we look to use adaptogens to help modulate some of the effects that a pharmaceutical would. But again, it's an individual journey. It's an individual basis. I'm just sharing with what I use and working with companies that I trust. So, thanks for watching and I know you're going to have a multitude of questions about supplements. So, if you drop a comment and have a question about a supplement, we'll try to get to it as fast as we can and give you some insight of where to go to find more information about clean supplements and what ones actually work for women because again, there's not a lot of research out there that shows there's efficacy for everything for women. So, again, thanks for watching and drop a comment here and there.

- Protein for Women Over 40: How to Find the Right Balance in Midlife: Kind: captions Language: en If you're a woman in midlife, you've probably heard this advice lately. Eat more protein. But how much do you actually need? And is it possible to eat too much? Because in midlife, protein matters, but balance matters even more. Today, we're talking about how to get the protein that your body needs without throwing the rest of your nutrition out of balance. Welcome to the Bloom and Beyond podcast. Before I begin, I want to let you know that the information shared in this episode is for educational andformational purposes only, and it's not attended intended as medical advice. It's not a substitute for professional medical diagnosis, treatment, or guidance. As always, consult with your physician or qualified health care provider before you make any changes to your diet, exercise, or health routine, especially if you have a medical condition or are taking medication. I have wellness and nutrition certifications and my goal is to empower you with information that you can discuss with your healthcare provider. So protein becomes incredibly important as we move through our 40s, 50s, and beyond. But what we don't talk about enough is more protein isn't always better. And protein alone isn't the full picture. >> [snorts] >> Today we're talking about how to balance protein in midlife, why your body needs more of it, how much is actually helpful, and how to make sure that you're not crowding out the other nutrients that your body depends on for energy, hormones, digestion, and overall health. Let's talk about protein the balanced way. So, why does protein matter more in midlife? As we age, our bodies naturally begin to lose muscle. This process is called sarcopenia and it starts as early as our 30s, but it accelerates after menopause. Estrogen plays a role in maintaining muscle mass. And when estrogen declines, muscle becomes easier to lose and harder to rebuild. Now, why does this matter? Because muscle is your metabolism. Muscle supports blood sugar balance. Muscle protects your bones and joints. Muscle helps you stay strong, independent, and energized. Protein is what your body uses to maintain and rebuild that muscle. But protein isn't just about muscle. In midlife, protein also helps stabilize blood sugar, reduce cravings, support hormone production, maintain healthy hair, skin, and nails. keep you fuller longer and support immune function. If you've ever felt like your energy crashes easily or you're hungry again an hour after eating, low protein might be part of the reason. Now, how much protein do women in midlife need? Most women in midlife need more than the minimum daily requirement. A helpful general range is 20 to 30 gram of protein per meal. That's roughly 80 to 100 gram per day for many women. That depends on body size and activity level, too. The key isn't eating a huge amount at dinner. The goal is spreading protein throughout the day. So for example, breakfast 20 to 25 gram, lunch 25 to 30 gram, dinner 25 to 30 gram, and an optional snack 10 to 20 gram. This helps your body use protein efficiently for muscle repair and blood sugar stability. The midlife mistake is going all in on protein. And this is where balance comes in. Because once women here eat more protein, many go all in. Protein shakes for breakfast, chicken and eggs all day, low carb, everything. Cutting out grains, fruit, and even healthy carbohydrates in the process. And this is where the problems can start because your body doesn't run on protein alone. When protein crowds out other nutrients, you may experience low energy, constipation, hormone imbalances, poor digestion, increased stress hormones, thyroid slowdown, and blood sugar swings. Your body in midlife needs balance, not extremes. Carbohydrates still matter. Carbohydrates have gotten a bad reputation. But for women in midlife, they're actually really important. Healthy carbohydrates support thyroid function, hormone balance, cortisol regulation, brain function and mood, sleep quality, and gut health. When carbs are too low, the body perceives stress and cortisol can rise, which may lead to belly fat, fatigue, and poor sleep. The key is choosing quality carbohydrates such as vegetables, fruit, beans and lentils, oats, quinoa, brown rice, and sweet potatoes. Protein works best with healthy carbohydrates, not instead of them. Now, we don't want to forget about fiber either. Another risk of high protein, low carb eating is low fiber. Fiber is critical for your gut health, estrogen metabolism, cholesterol balance, blood sugar control, and feeling full and satisfied. So, women in midlife should aim for about 25 to 30 grams of fiber per day. And most fiber comes from plant foods, vegetables, fruit, whole grains, beans, nuts, seeds. If your diet's mostly protein and very little plant food, then your gut and your hormones may struggle. Healthy fats also play a role, too. Protein often gets the spotlight, but healthy fats are just as important. Healthy fats support hormone production, brain health, joint health, skin, and hair, and absorption of vitamins A, D, E, and K. include sources like avocado, olive oil, nuts and seeds, and fatty fish like salmon. Again, balance is crucial. Now, let's talk about what balanced protein looks like in real life. So, instead of building your meal around protein, think protein plus fiber plus healthy carbs plus healthy fat. So, for example, breakfast, Greek yogurt, berries, chia seeds, and a handful of granola. Watch the sugar, though. Lunch: salmon salad with mixed greens, quinoa, olive oil, and vegetables. Dinner, chicken, roasted sweet potatoes, and a big portion of roasted or steamed vegetables. And if you desire a snack, apple with peanut butter or a protein smoothie with spinach and fruit. This kind of balance will support muscle, energy, digestion, hormones, and long-term health. These are signs that your protein intake may be too low. You feel hungry shortly after meals. You're losing strength. You have low energy. You crave sugar or carbs constantly. Your hair is thinning or your nails are brittle. In signs your diet may be too protein heavy or constipation, low energy or fatigue, poor sleep, feeling wired or stressed, little fruit, grains or plant foods in your diet. So, we need a midlife nutrition mindset. If there's one thing that I want you to take away from today, it's this. Midlife nutrition isn't about extremes. It's about balance and consistency. You do not need a high protein diet. You need a balanced diet with adequate protein. The goal isn't to chase numbers or follow the latest trend. The goal is to support your body so you feel strong, steady, energized, clearheaded, and wellnourished. So, here are some simple protein tips for midlife. First, add protein to your breakfast. Don't wait until dinner. Two, aim for 20 to 30 grams per meal. Three, include plant foods at every meal. Four, do not eliminate healthy carbohydrates. Five, pair protein with fiber and healthy fats. And six, focus on real food first, supplement second. So in closing, your body in midlife is changing. And when you give it the right balance of protein, fiber, healthy fats, and whole food carbohydrates, it responds. You'll have more energy, better blood sugar, stronger muscles, and better mood and focus. Not because you restricted more, but because you nourished more. So at Bloom and Beyond, this is our focus. supporting your body with what it truly needs so you can feel strong, confident, and well in this stage of life. If this episode was helpful, please be sure to follow the podcast and share it with a friend who's trying to figure out what health what healthy really looks like in midlife. And please like this episode and start following us to learn more about living healthy in midlife. And remember, you don't need perfection, you just need balance. I'll see you next time on Bloom and Beyond.

- Stop Copying Morning Routines. Do This Instead.: Kind: captions Language: en Let's clear this up. This is not another video telling you to copy an entrepreneur's 4:00 a.m. routine or do cold plunges and 25 things before breakfast because most people are doing morning routines completely wrong. They follow someone else's checklist and call it discipline. But a real morning routine is not about productivity. It's about brain science. And once you understand how your brain actually wakes up, you'll stop copying routines and start creating your [Music] own. Most people have the wrong idea about morning routines. They think it's about waking up at 4:00 a.m. uh crushing 15 tasks before breakfast and feeling unstoppable. But that's not a routine. That's a performance. A real morning routine isn't about productivity. It's about brain science. And to understand that, you need to understand how your brain actually wakes up. Because throughout the day, your brain operates on different frequencies like tuning into different radio stations. Each frequency puts you in a different mental state and supports a different kind of activity. Each morning when you wake up, your brain gently moves from delta and theta into alpha. This is a natural transition. And during this time, your body also starts to release cortisol, your natural wake up hormone. A mild cortisol rises healthy. It helps you get alert and moving. Your body is built to wake up gradually. A gentle rise in cortisol is your body's natural alarm clock. It helps you get out of bed and start the day. But when you wake up and immediately check emails or scroll Instagram or rush into a Zoom call, your brain skips the calm stage and goes into crisis mode. It's like going from 0 to 100 in seconds. Your cortisol spikes and your nervous system thinks there is an emergency. Now your body is on edge. Your heart is racing. Breath is shallow, thoughts are scattered. And if this becomes your morning pattern, your brain starts expecting stress the moment you wake up. That's why by 10:00 a.m. your mind already feels like it's running a marathon. You can't focus. You feel restless, distracted, like your brain spins. Even when nothing urgent happens, and by 2 p.m. you get exhausted. Not because your day was intense, but because your nervous system burned half your energy before the day even began. You didn't wake up tired. You just started your day in stress mode and your systems got exhausted. So this is where the real job of a morning routine comes into play to help your brain move gently from rest to rhythm, from alpha to beta, from dream mode to doing mode without shocking your system. When this transition is done right, your brain feels supported, your body feels safe, and your mind can wake up without rushing. But this is where most people get wrong. They overload them. They wake up at 4, run 5 kilometers, take a cold bath, journal, meditate, manifest, stretch, eat protein, do it all. By 9:00 a.m., they're not energized. They're already drained. See, a good morning routine isn't supposed to exhaust you. It's supposed to energize you. So if you're wondering, okay, then what actually works? I will tell you four fundamental but high return on investment habits that not only boosts your energy but also supports your mental and physical health. Number one, sunlight. Step outside and get natural light at least 20 minutes in the morning. This anchors your body's internal clock and regulates your cortisol, the hormone that controls your alertness, mood, and stress. Number two, movement. Walk, stretch, dance, do yoga, anything that gets your body moving. This signals safety to your nervous system and kickstarts your energy for the day. Number three, journaling. Empty your mind onto paper. It clears mental fog, helps your emotional brain process leftover thoughts from sleep, and gives your day a cleaner start. Number four, meditation or breath work. Even 5 minutes can settle your nervous system. It activates your parasympathetic nervous system, the rest and digest mode and sets a calm foundation for the day. Having said that, this is the golden rule of the morning routine. You choose the activities that give you energy, not what looks impressive. If journaling drains you, skip it. If running makes you tired in the morning, don't do it. Test different practices. Stick on to the ones that leave you feeling calm, clear, and awake. Give your brain a gentle warm-up. And when you tune your mind right, the rest of the day moves in rhythm. That's the real point of a morning routine. A good morning routine will help you start your day with clarity. You feel grounded, not rushed. Your thoughts are sharper. Your emotions are steadier. Your body feels lighter and more alive. Find your morning routine. It has the power to change how the rest of your day feels.

- 5 Women Entrepreneurs Share Their Secrets To Success | Refinery29: Kind: captions Language: en Inspiration can come from any direction. But how do you take that new concept and make it a reality? In advance of the Inc. Women’s Summit, I’m meeting up with some of my favorite entrepreneurs to talk about what empowers them on a daily basis. You have all created businesses from out-of-the-box thinking. What made you veer left when everybody else was going right? Bobbi. When I moved to New York, it was the ‘80s. And everyone was doing the over made up contour look. I know it’s back. I still don’t like contour. So I would make up the models to make them look like they weren’t wearing makeup. And that was my calling card. But there was no makeup available that allowed you to do that. I met a chemist one day at a shoot and I said I can’t find a lipstick that didn’t smell, wasn’t greasy, and I wanted to match lips. Then I realized, “Wow. This is something.” For me, entrepreneurship, it wasn’t something that I aspired to. I always wanted to be an inventor. If I can be the perfect balance between Beyoncé and Bill Nye the Science Guy every single day… Now we’re installing our technologies around different countries in emerging markets. Turning everything from floors and roads to baby strollers into energy-generating solutions. You guys both touched on this idea of identifying problems that existed in your life and looking at ways that you could shape an industry, rather than looking at the industry itself. I started a fashion technology company with my co-founder. We had no fashion or technology experience. We knew what it meant to buy a dress for $1,000, wear it once, and then be photographed on social media. And feel like you could never wear it again. So we were like, “Well, that doesn’t make sense.” Renting dresses made sense. If you thought too far down the road, it could be easy to find all the why not’s versus the how can you make this happen. I want to hear Angela’s story talking about how you are helping young entrepreneurs. So I started NewME, which is an accelerator for minorities in 2011. We focus only on the technology industry, which at the time, it was a young, technical, white guys’ club. I did not really start NewME as a business. It was a problem that I wanted to solve. And I thought it was going to be like a one-time project. What do you see is standing in between these women and the dollars that you know they need to succeed? When I work with men, they are not scared to ask for money even if what they’re working on is mediocre. But women can have something phenomenal but it’s like we hold ourselves back. So a lot of the work that I do in particular with women is confidence building, is getting them comfortable with asking for the money. Priya, I want to talk about the impact you are having on actual people: helping them create wealth in the way that they've never even thought of before. Historically, financial planning has been reserved for the wealthy. And when I was working on Wall Street, my clients were old and rich. And all of my friends at the same time were wondering where do we go for the answers. There’s this completely underserved kind of ignored demographic that we call Henrys, high earners not rich yet. I so often hear young women say, “I just want to be paid what I’m worth.” I don’t even know what that means. And I very rarely hear young women say, “I want to make a lot of money.” Right? It’s as if we all sort of internalize this idea that the budget’s not there. Research show that men are four times more likely than women to ask. So at Stash Wealth, salary negotiations come up. Set it as a goal though and it has to be a very concrete goal. Like I want to buy a $250,000 house in three years so I want to get a raise and let’s back into what you need to do today to be on track. The flipside that millennials have to be very careful of is entitlement. For example, one kid said, “I feel like I really need to make x.” I’m like, “Oh, that’s interesting. For this job, if you do all of this research, this is the highest you could get paid.” And he’s like, “Yeah, I didn’t really think about what the job was. I just kind of thought New York.” What are you saying to me? I don’t think women talk about comp and salaries in the same way men do. Women I advise, some of the most valuable help I give them is actually talking in a real way about what you should be making. But getting the data out there— that’s hard especially amongst women because they’re fewer women who are in leadership roles. I find younger women are much more transparent with their salary. For them it’s not TMI, it’s cocktail talk. And I think that that lifting the veil —is going to get us closer to equality. –I think that’s really important. In “The Big Life,” I have an entire chapter devoted to the side hustle secret to success. How do you know when it’s time to make your side hustle your main hustle? I fully believe in the side hustle and in fact, when I worked in finance, because I was not passionate about finance, I would spend whatever time I had doing college essay editing, which I kind of made all the business around. But ultimately went to business school and co-founded Rent the Runway in business school. So I was kind of side hustling it. I was taking classes but I’m a pretty risk averse entrepreneur so the whole way, I had a timeline of milestones we had to meet. And I think it was helpful for me and my co-founder, kept us honest. Ultimately one of those milestones was raising money. And for me, raising money was another way of checking myself on. Was this a good concept that VC’s would put their name behind? So I very much believe that parallel processing, kind of always having a little bit of a safety net, and just being honest with yourself if something’s not working. And forcing that brutal honesty, it’s hard. You need to be able to sell something or it’s not a business. Don’t quit your job. Don’t do anything until you figure that out. And then even after you figure that out, you got to do a deeper dive into the customer and try to find more customers like the ones who gave you a dollar. When I’m talking to young women, they will ask me, “How do you get ahead in a male-dominated industry if you’re a woman?” The advice I always give is to forget you’re a woman, right? You are a person with a good idea. Just say it. That was exactly the advice that I gave myself was just to remember that you’re there like any other employee and to speak up when you have a great idea. I did get called in once. My boss told me that my attitude with my colleagues was abrasive. And I wasn’t sure if it was because I was a female. I’ve been in rooms with investors who are supposed to be investing in women and people of color and have the investor say, “So who’s the tech lead on this?” I’m like, “It’s me.” “Well, who came up with this?” I’m like, “Ask me a question first and see if I don’t have the answer.” And then all of a sudden they’re just like, “So did your father help you…” “Did my father, what?” I actually disagree that you should forget that you’re a woman. Every single day I get up being like, “Jessica, you are a black woman. You’re going to have to do three times as much to get half as far.” Is anyone besides me been called difficult? Oh yeah. I love it though. Me too. It’s a compliment. When I was younger, I didn’t like it. Maybe I should not say stuff back as much. But the older I got, I’m like I kind of like that about me. We all know that we can’t just be like one of the boys that—but the good news is that we have our special perspective. You embrace the difference, right? I don’t think a man could have started Rent the Runway. There’s this old idea that women are in competition with each other. That there’s backstabbing. That there’s only room at the table for one woman. But I’ve been so inspired by how millennial women have replaced competition with collaboration. Being someone who has been in the industry for a long time, when I was first starting my company especially with other makeup artists, you would never give any advice. Never would they ever tell you —because everyone was a—right. —I was about to say. Everyone was in competition. And now as I’m reinventing myself and need some different things, they’re here. Call this person. Do this. This is where you get this. It’s an amazing change. It is. This year I turned 60 and I started a new lifestyle brand. There’s more and more opportunity. And there’s a million things you’re going to end up doing. On your own terms. Be the entrepreneur of your life.

- Nancy Griffin: Empowering Women Through Wealth and Wellness: Kind: captions Language: en You've lived a great life and done well for yourself, but what mark will you leave on the world? Now, here are your hosts, Stan and Katie Beth. >> Welcome back to the Your Life, Your Legacy podcast with your host, Katie Bethand. My guest today is the CEO and founder of Women Worth and Wellness, Nancy Griffin. Nancy, thank you so much for jumping on with me today. >> Katie, thanks so much for inviting me. This is a real pleasure. >> Absolutely. So before we dive in and talk a lot about women worth and wellness, I was hoping you would take a few minutes and tell our listeners a little bit about your background and really kind of what inspired you to start this organization. So tell us a little bit about you, Nancy. >> Okay. Well, I won't go into the first career in all those configurations, but I was recruited into wealth management by this woman who basically did a 180 degree. I was headed in one direction. We were having coffee and she said, "Nancy, I don't know why you think you want to spend 20 of your next years doing that. I think you should take a look at wealth management." because she'd been given a bagload of money as a VP of a firm that had been outplaced and went seeking advice and fell very short of what she felt was ideal. So, she was a wealth manager, recruited me, and of course meet my boss, met her boss, he and I got along just fine. He said, "Well, you've got to go and get some some credentials first, pass a few tests, get a few licenses, and away we go." And then of of course as soon as you arrive into a great firm, they'll do all sorts of training and coaching. But at the end of the day, you've got to go find your own clients. And they tell you the most important thing is to find clients to whom you feel you can add the most amount of value. And this was in the '9s. This is just when women were really starting to earn a whole lot more and really starting to pay attention to putting that whole primarily an investment plan. you know, I ramped it up and I call it a wealth plan, but we're really, really interested in that. And so that's what I did. And one of the investment groups that I worked with had a whole program targeting women. And so I took their program and did a first event one Saturday afternoon in November, way back when, and I had 172, I think, women show up because everybody was so keen. This was kind of a new topic. And so you work that group until you've got clients on board with you. But then as you sit and work with these individual women, there's a new aha. And the new aa is that women are kind of the nurse practitioner in their family. They're expected to know about everybody's well-being and how much knowledge do they have and how much time do they have to go get that knowledge. And so that's when I decided to partner with notfor-profit health related organizations that were focusing on women. Uh for example, in Toronto, we have a hospital called Women's College Hospital. It's over 100 years old. And so that was a perfect partnership because they had a lot of tremendous resources and they were really taking the approach on we want to know more about women. Not women as little men. We want to know more about women. The other thing is is this heart disease is the leading cause of death for women, not cancer. And so did an event with them and of course some of my clients came and brought their friends and but every time, so this is kind of a long answer to your question. Every time I hosted one of these events, I would charge a nominal amount like $25 or $50 and it would go to that notfor-profit organization. So then I can show women how that charitable donation enhances their net worth. A very small donation, very small impact, but when you're doing your tax return, it's really nice to have deductions like that to minimize the amount of tax that you're paying. And then also we also know that once we're in a situation where we give or we share, we feel good. So these women were, you know, starting to understand the whole scenario around uh net worth and self-worth, charitable giving. And of course, fast forward 30 plus years later and women have a whole lot more wealth to steward. And of course, as you well know, they're starting to inherit lots of wealth with a whole lot more to come. So it's transformational philanthropy. It's really women's power at its finest, but a lot of women kind of are like, "Oh my goodness, you know, now what?" >> Right. Yeah. It can be overwhelming. And it's interesting because you've been in in this business and kind of doing this part of the piece of the program for such a long time. You've seen how, you know, there was already a need for this, you know, whenever you started 20, 30 years ago. And now I feel like that need is just expanded. Like you said, there's women that have built their own wealth. There's a lot of women that are inheriting wealth, especially coming from this baby boomer generation. We're on the estate planning side of things. So, that's a lot of what we deal with. And women have kind of were taking over in the workplace as well. And so, there are just a whole bunch of different pieces in there. And most things, whether it's in in business or in the health industry, it's really geared naturally towards men. And so I thought it was interesting that you pointed out the fact that for women it's actually heart disease that is our number one concern. So all of this history, all this background, it's what inspired you to go out and start this business. And so tell our listeners who may not have heard about Women Worth and Wellness, you know, exactly what type of services and training and leadership coaching and what type of things do does women worth and wellness provide? >> Okay. Well, when I started, I created this I started this whole focus in 1992. In 1994, I trademarked the brand. If we go back to my first career, I was with Proctor and Gamble. So, you know, the power of a brand. And I also was in a strategic coaching sort of a a group situation where business owners were being coached. And one of the things that they emphasized to the max was your unique ability. Everybody has unique ability. Know what it is. define it and define it in terms of how you can bring forward that to the max. So the women worth and wellness whole idea came from all of that sort of happening all at the same time. But the other thing was as I spent more time with women going back to the health component there was really no point in building a wealth plan if you haven't focused on getting the health scenarios out of the way to begin with. For example, mental health is huge. We know that and if you don't have the right mindset for your wealth plan, then it's very difficult to have that conversation. We also know that if you are not healthy, you're not going anywhere anytime soon. And so making health the first order of business was really something that I focused on as I started to work with a lot of different people. And then as I developed wealth plans, I built that health aspect into the wealth plan because an excellent wealth plan will have anticipated all sorts of challenges going forward. You know, number one is retirement. Will you have enough money to support what you want in retirement? What if a critical illness pops up in the interimm? Are you protected? Are you going to have to draw down from your investment portfolio? What about a disability? What about premature death? So it really really became a very holistic brand and the more people started to realize about the importance of good health and the opportunities that philanthropy present the more you can circle back and invest back in health as it pertains to what you believe is most important. You know another example is Parkinson's for example. Some families have been stricken with Parkinson's. Now you realize it's like, well, where the heck did that come from? And is this something that's going to affect me? Well, you know what? I really want to start supporting the Parkinson's organization with to help them with more research dollars so they can lower the microscope and hopefully make things better for a lot of people going for it. I love it because I love your holistic approach to women and wealth and the fact that you've built I think that missing piece that a lot of people miss is the aspect of really thinking through health and I love what you just said about that relating directly to philanthropy. You know, if you have a history of breast cancer in your family, if you have a history of Parkinson's or type 1 diabetes, whatever it might be, there are some great opportunities out there for you to use your wealth and your influence to really make a positive impact, not only for other people here, but you know, possibly for your future generations and your family as well.
